explanation: There will be 3 swaps in first iteration and 1 swap in second iteration.

stem: In doubly linked lists

options.label: A
options.text: A pointer is maintained to store both next and previous nodes.

options.label: B
options.text: Two pointers are maintained to store next and previous nodes.

options.label: C
options.text: A pointer to self is maintained for each node.

explanation: A single pointer variable holds exactly one address, so it cannot reference both neighbours at once. Each node of a doubly linked list therefore carries two separate pointer fields, next and prev, which is option B.

stem: From a complete graph, by removing maximum _________ edges, we can construct a spanning tree.
explanation: A spanning tree on n vertices has exactly n - 1 edges, so from a graph with e edges you delete e - (n - 1) = e - n + 1 of them. Removing even one more would drop the edge count below n - 1 and disconnect the graph.

stem: If the array is already sorted, which of these algorithms will exhibit the best performance
explanation: On an already sorted array insertion sort makes one comparison per element and zero shifts, giving Theta(n), its best case. Merge sort and heap sort are Theta(n log n) on every input, and quicksort with a first or last element pivot degrades to Theta(n^2) on sorted data, so insertion sort wins.

stem: Which of the following algorithm is not stable?
explanation: A sort is stable if records with equal keys keep their original relative order. Quicksort's partition step swaps elements across arbitrary distances and can therefore invert two equal keys, whereas bubble, insertion, and merge sort only move an element past a strictly greater one, so they preserve equal-key order.

stem: Which of the following has search efficiency of O(1)
explanation: A hash table computes the bucket index directly from the key, so an average-case lookup is O(1) given a good hash function and a bounded load factor. A balanced tree search is O(log n), a linked-list search is O(n), and a heap only gives O(1) access to the min or max, not to an arbitrary key.

stem: Which one of the below mentioned is linear data structure –
explanation: A structure is linear when its elements sit in a single sequence, each with at most one predecessor and one successor. Arrays, stacks, and queues all satisfy that (they differ only in where insertion and removal are allowed), unlike trees and graphs, so the answer is all of the above.

stem: Time complexity of Depth First Traversal of is
explanation: With an adjacency-list representation DFS marks each of the |V| vertices visited exactly once and scans each vertex's adjacency list once, so every edge is examined once in a directed graph and twice in an undirected one. Total work is therefore Theta(|V| + |E|), not Theta(|V|) or Theta(|E|) alone, since a graph can be edge-heavy or vertex-heavy.

stem: In context with time-complexity, find the odd out –
explanation: Deleting a node from a linked list (given the pointer), searching a hash table (average case) and adding an edge in an adjacency matrix (set M[i][j] = 1) are all O(1). Heapify is the odd one out: sifting one node into place costs O(log n), and building a heap from n elements costs O(n).

stem: Shell sort uses
explanation: Shell sort is a gapped insertion sort: for each gap h it insertion-sorts the sub-sequences of elements h positions apart, and the final pass with h = 1 is an ordinary insertion sort on the whole array. It never uses merge, selection or quick sort.

stem: All possible spanning trees of graph G

options.label: A
options.text: Have same number of edges and vertices.

options.label: B
options.text: Have same number of edges and but not vertices.

options.label: C
options.text: Have same number of vertices but not edges.

explanation: A spanning tree of a connected graph with n vertices must include all n vertices and be acyclic, which forces exactly n - 1 edges. So every spanning tree of G has the same vertex count (n) and the same edge count (n - 1), regardless of whether Prim or Kruskal produced it.

stem: Which of the following algorithm cannot be designed without recursion –
explanation: Every problem which can be solved using recursion can also be solved using iterations.

stem: Which of the following uses memorization?
explanation: Memoization means storing the result of each subproblem the first time it is computed and reusing it instead of recomputing. That is exactly what dynamic programming does with its table; greedy makes one locally best choice and never revisits subproblems, and plain divide and conquer recomputes overlapping subproblems rather than caching them.

stem: Heap is an example of:
explanation: A heap is a complete binary tree (every level filled left to right except possibly the last) with a heap order property: each parent is greater than or equal to its children in a max-heap, or less than or equal in a min-heap. It is not a binary search tree, because the ordering is only parent-to-child and says nothing about left versus right siblings.

stem: What do we use Dijkstra algorithm for?

options.label: A
options.text: Find the number of vertices there are in a given graph.

options.label: B
options.text: Find the shortest path between two vertices on a weighted graph.

options.label: C
options.text: Find the number of edges there are in a given graph.

options.label: D
options.text: Find the path between a single node, on a weighted graph, to all other nodes in the graph.

explanation: Dijkstra is a single-source shortest path algorithm: starting from one source it repeatedly settles the unvisited vertex with the smallest tentative distance and relaxes its outgoing edges, ending with the shortest path from that source to every other vertex on a non-negative weighted graph. Option B is too narrow, since a single run produces the distances to all nodes, not just to one target.

stem: Which of the following Boolean equations describes the action of Fig. 2.5.2?
explanation: In Fig. 2.5.2 the top gate is an AND with a bubble (NAND) on A and B, giving NOT(A AND B), the bottom gate is an AND on B and C, giving B AND C, and the OR gate combines them: X = NOT(A AND B) + (B AND C). Option C is the De Morgan trap, since NOT(A AND B) = NOT A + NOT B, not NOT A AND NOT B.

stem: Which of the following Boolean equations describes the truth table in Fig. 2.5.4?
explanation: The truth table in Fig. 2.5.4 gives X = 0, 0, 1, 1 for AB = 00, 01, 10, 11, so X simply equals A. By the absorption law A AND (A + B) = A, so option B reproduces the table; A + B fails on AB = 01 (gives 1, table says 0), (A AND NOT B) + B fails on AB = 01, and A + NOT B fails on AB = 00.

stem: What is the Boolean expression that can be obtained for the Karnaugh map cell indicated in Fig. 2.5.7?
explanation: The marked cell in Fig. 2.5.7 sits in column AB = 10 and row CD = 01, so A = 1, B = 0, C = 0, D = 1. The minterm for those values is A AND NOT B AND NOT C AND D.

stem: Refer to Fig. 2.5.7 (above). What is the minimum number of cell groups that can be obtained from the Karnaugh map using logic 1 cells only?
explanation: The map has eight 1s. Cell (AB = 10, CD = 01) has only one 1-neighbour (AB = 00, CD = 01, via column wraparound) and cell (AB = 10, CD = 10) has only one 1-neighbour (AB = 11, CD = 10), so each of those must sit in its own pair, and neither pair can grow to a quad. That forces at least: the quad B AND NOT D (AB = 01/11 with CD = 00/10), those two pairs, and one more pair to pick up (AB = 00, CD = 00), giving four groups; an exhaustive check confirms no three-group cover exists.

stem: Which logic function does the circuit in Fig. 2.5.6 perform?
explanation: Fig. 2.5.6 is the classic four-NAND network: N1 = NOT(A AND B), N2 = NOT(A AND N1), N3 = NOT(B AND N1), X = NOT(N2 AND N3) = A AND NOT B, OR NOT A AND B. That output is 0, 1, 1, 0 for AB = 00, 01, 10, 11, which is XOR.

stem: What type of circuit is illustrated in to Fig 4.5.1?
explanation: In Fig. 4.5.1 the two NAND gates take (C, A) and (NOT C, B), and the output NAND gives X = (A AND C) + (B AND NOT C): C selects A when C = 1 and B when C = 0. That is a 2-to-1 multiplexer, which is the same thing as a data selector.

stem: The time required for a gate or inverter to change its state is called:
explanation: Propagation time (propagation delay) is the interval between a change at a gate input and the resulting change at its output, normally measured between the 50 percent points of the two waveforms. Rise time is the 10 to 90 percent transition of a pulse and decay time the 90 to 10 percent transition, so both describe the shape of one edge, not the input-to-output response of the gate.

stem: The number of full and half-adders required to add 16-bit numbers is:
explanation: The one half-adder can add the least significant bit of the two numbers. Full adders are required to add the remaining 15 bits as they all involve adding carries.

stem: A demultiplexer is used to:

options.label: A
options.text: Route the data from single input to one of many outputs

options.label: D
options.text: Select data from several inputs and route it to single output

explanation: A demultiplexer routes its single input line to one of many output lines chosen by the select lines, which is option A. If bits arrive serially on that input while the select lines are advanced by a counter, each bit is latched onto a different output line, so the demux also performs serial to parallel conversion, which is option B. Option D describes a multiplexer, so the answer is C, both A and B.

stem: In ______ each station sends a frame whenever it has a frame to send.
explanation: In pure ALOHA a station transmits as soon as it has a frame, with no synchronization, and collisions are detected by the absence of an acknowledgement. Slotted ALOHA forces the station to wait for the start of the next time slot before transmitting, so it does not fit 'whenever it has a frame to send'. Hence only pure ALOHA (A) is correct.

stem: The Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) is a communications protocol used for:

options.label: A
options.text: Assisting the users to configure multiple network devices from a single source

options.label: B
options.text: Mapping of Internet layer addresses into link layer addresse

options.label: C
options.text: Assisting the users by helping to link between common names to an IP address and back.

options.label: D
options.text: Transmitting files between computers on the internet over TCP/IP connections

explanation: ARP broadcasts a request asking which host owns a given IPv4 address and receives that host's MAC address in reply, so it maps internet-layer (IP) addresses to link-layer (MAC) addresses, which is option B. Option A describes DHCP, option C describes DNS, and option D describes FTP.

stem: Which network protocol uses out-of-band control?
explanation: FTP opens a control connection on TCP port 21 for commands and replies and a separate data connection (port 20 in active mode) for the file contents, so its control information travels on a different connection from the data: that is out-of-band control. HTTP, SMTP and TCP all carry their control information in the same connection or byte stream as the data, so only FTP (D) qualifies.

stem: When both tables can have only one record on either side of the relationship, this is known as:
explanation: A one-to-one relationship means each row in the first table corresponds to at most one row in the second and vice versa, which is exactly 'only one record on either side'. One-to-many permits many rows on one side, and many-to-many permits many on both.

stem: Activity diagram, use case diagram, collaboration diagram and sequence diagram are considered as types of:
explanation: UML splits diagrams into structural (class, object, component, deployment, package) and behavioral ones. Activity, use case, sequence and collaboration diagrams all describe how the system behaves or how objects interact over time rather than its static make-up, so they are behavioral diagrams (D).

stem: Kind of diagrams which are used to show interactions between series of messages are classified as:
explanation: A collaboration (communication) diagram is an interaction diagram: it draws the objects and the links between them and labels each link with numbered messages, showing the series of messages exchanged. Activity and state chart diagrams model control flow and object states rather than message exchange, and 'object lifeline diagram' is not a UML diagram type (a lifeline is a component of a sequence diagram), so C is correct.

stem: Diagrams which are used to distribute files, libraries and tables across topology of hardware are called:
explanation: A deployment diagram maps artifacts (executables, libraries, files, database tables) onto the hardware nodes and communication paths they run on, which is precisely distributing them across the hardware topology. Use case, sequence and collaboration diagrams describe behavior and interaction, not physical placement, so A is correct.

stem: Dynamic aspects related to a system are shown with help of:
explanation: Interaction diagrams, the family that contains sequence and collaboration diagrams, show objects exchanging messages over time and so capture the dynamic aspects of a system. A sequence diagram is only one member of that family, and deployment and use case diagrams show hardware topology and external actor requirements, so the general category B is the correct choice.

stem: Class diagram, component diagram, object diagram and deployment diagram are considered as types of
explanation: Class, component, object and deployment diagrams all describe the static make-up of the system: its classes, its runtime objects, its software components and the hardware nodes they sit on. Static make-up is what UML calls a structural diagram, so A is correct; behavioral diagrams (activity, use case, sequence, state chart) describe what the system does over time.

stem: Weak entities are represented in UML diagrams by using aggregations called
explanation: UML has no dedicated weak-entity notation, so the identifying relationship is modelled as a qualified aggregation: the qualifier is the weak entity's partial key, which identifies it only within its owning (identifying) entity. A plain non-qualified aggregation would not carry that partial key, and 'segregation' is not a UML concept, so D is correct.

stem: In UML diagrams, relationship between object and component parts is represented by
explanation: Aggregation is UML's whole-part relationship: it connects an object to the component parts it is assembled from. Ordination, segregation and increment are not UML relationship types, so B is correct.

stem: What is a hash table?

options.label: A
options.text: A structure that maps values to keys.

options.label: B
options.text: A structure that maps keys to values.

options.label: D
options.text: A structure used to implement stack and queue.

explanation: A hash table is used to implement an associative array, which has a key-value pair. So, the hash table maps keys to values.

stem: If several elements are competing for the same bucket in the hash table, what is it called?
explanation: When the hash function sends two or more distinct keys to the same bucket index, the keys compete for that bucket and the event is called a collision. Collisions are unavoidable once the key space exceeds the table size and are handled by chaining or open addressing, so C is correct.

stem: In a hash table, what is direct addressing?

options.label: A
options.text: Distinct array position for every possible key.

explanation: Direct addressing is possible only when we can afford to allocate an array that has one position for every possible key.

stem: What is the search complexity in direct addressing?
explanation: Since every key has a unique array position, searching takes a constant time.

stem: What is a hash function?

options.label: A
options.text: A function has allocated memory to keys.

options.label: B
options.text: A function that computes the location of the key in the array.

options.label: C
options.text: A function that creates an array.

explanation: In a hash table, there are fewer array positions than the keys, so the position of the key in the array has to be computed, this is done using the hash function.

stem: What can be the techniques to avoid collision?

options.label: A
options.text: Make the hash function appear random.

explanation: All three listed items act against collisions: a hash function that behaves randomly stops any regular pattern of keys from piling into one slot, simple uniform hashing makes each of the m slots equally likely for every key, and chaining holds all keys that land on the same slot in a list at that slot. Since A, B and C are each valid, D is the answer.

stem: What is the load factor?
explanation: In simple chaining, load factor is the average number of elements stored in a chain, and is given by the ratio of number of elements stored to the number of slots in the array.

stem: What is simple uniform hashing?

options.label: A
options.text: Every element has equal probability of hashing into any of the slots.

options.label: B
options.text: A weighted probabilistic method is used to hash elements into the slots.

explanation: In simple uniform hashing, any given element is equally likely to hash into any of the slots available in the array.

stem: In simple uniform hashing, what is the search complexity?
explanation: Under simple uniform hashing with chaining, both successful and unsuccessful searches cost Theta(1 + alpha): constant work to compute the hash and index the slot, plus the expected chain length alpha = n/m. When the table is sized proportional to the number of keys, alpha is a constant, so the search is O(1).

stem: In simple chaining, what data structure is appropriate?
explanation: Chaining wants deletion of an element to be O(1) once you hold a pointer to its node, and a doubly linked list allows that node to be spliced out directly using its prev and next pointers. In a singly linked list you would first have to walk the chain to find the predecessor, making deletion proportional to the chain length.

stem: Which of the following gives a logical structure of the database graphically?
explanation: The entity-relationship diagram is the graphical notation of the E-R model: entity sets are drawn as rectangles, relationship sets as diamonds, and lines connect them, so the diagram shows the logical structure of the database as a picture. The other three names are not standard E-R notations.

stem: The entity relationship set is represented in E-R diagram as:
explanation: In E-R notation a relationship set is drawn as a diamond, joined by lines to the rectangles of the entity sets it relates. Double diamonds are used only for the identifying relationship set of a weak entity, and dashed lines only attach attributes to a relationship set, so neither of those is the plain relationship set.

stem: The Rectangles divided into two parts represents:
explanation: The first part of the rectangle contains the name of the entity set. The second part contains the names of all the attributes of the entity set.

stem: Consider a directed line (→) from the relationship set advisor to both entity sets instructor and student. This indicates __________ cardinality.
explanation: An arrow pointing at an entity set means at most one entity of that set takes part in the relationship, so arrows into both instructor and student mean an instructor advises at most one student and a student has at most one advisor, which is one to one.

stem: We indicate roles in E-R diagrams by labeling the lines that connect ____________ to ___________.
explanation: A role label distinguishes the parts an entity set plays when the same entity set participates more than once in one relationship set, and the label is written on the line that joins the relationship-set diamond to the entity-set rectangle, hence diamond to rectangle.

stem: An entity set that does not have sufficient attributes to form a primary key is termed a ___________.
explanation: An entity set whose own attributes are enough to form a primary key is a strong entity set; one that lacks such attributes cannot be identified by itself and is called a weak entity set, which is why it must borrow identification from an owning entity set.

stem: For a weak entity set to be meaningful, it must be associated with another entity set, called the:
explanation: A weak entity set has no primary key of its own, so it is existence dependent on another entity set whose key, combined with the weak entity's discriminator, identifies it; in standard E-R terminology that set is the identifying entity set.

stem: Weak entity set is represented as:
explanation: In standard ER notation a weak entity set is drawn as a double rectangle, which is why D is correct. The distractors are the other ER symbols: a double diamond is the identifying relationship of the weak entity, an underline marks a primary key, and a double line marks total participation in a relationship.

stem: If you were collecting and storing information about your music collection, an album would be considered a(n) ______.
explanation: An album is a real-world object you store data about and it has its own properties (title, artist, year), so in ER terms it is an entity. One specific album would be an instance of it, an attribute is a single property such as title, and a relation is the table the entity maps onto in the relational model.

stem: What term is used to refer to a specific record in your music database; for instance; information stored about a specific album?
explanation: One specific stored record, the data held about one particular album, is a single instance (occurrence) of the Album entity, so B is correct. Relation and Table both name the whole collection of album records and Column names one attribute, so none of those refers to a single record.

stem: Average memory access time can be calculated as:

options.label: A
options.text: Average memory access time = Hit time + (Miss rate * Miss penalty).

options.label: B
options.text: Average memory access time = Hit time – (Miss rate * Miss penalty).

options.label: C
options.text: Average memory access time = Hit time + (Miss rate / Miss penalty).

options.label: D
options.text: Average memory access time = Miss rate / Miss penalty.

explanation: Every access pays the hit time, and only the miss-rate fraction of accesses additionally pays the miss penalty, so AMAT = hit time + (miss rate * miss penalty). For example a 1 ns hit time with a 5% miss rate and a 100 ns miss penalty gives 1 + 0.05 * 100 = 6 ns.

stem: What is the throughput of simple/pure ALOHA?
explanation: Pure ALOHA throughput is S = G * e^(-2G), which peaks at G = 0.5 giving S = 1/(2e) = 0.184, i.e. 18.4%, and to the nearest whole percent that is the 18% option. Slotted ALOHA halves the vulnerable period and peaks at 1/e = 36.8%, so C and D are the slotted-ALOHA distractors, while 18.8% corresponds to no ALOHA result at all.

stem: What will be the output of the following C++ code?
explanation: The semicolon immediately after for (i = 0; i < 10; i++) makes the loop body EMPTY, so the loop simply runs i up to 10 and stops. The following braces are then just an ordinary block that executes once, printing the final value of i, so the output is 10 rather than 0123456789.

stem: What is the effect of writing a break statement inside a loop?
explanation: break exits the enclosing loop immediately, so all remaining iterations are cancelled. The trap is option B: skipping one iteration and carrying on is continue, not break; and break ends only the loop, not the whole program (that would be exit or return from main).

stem: Which of the following is not logical operator?
explanation: & is the BITWISE AND operator: it combines operands bit by bit. The three logical operators in C/C++ are && (AND), || (OR) and ! (NOT); unlike &, the logical && also short-circuits.

stem: In mathematics and computer programming, which is the correct order of mathematical operators? (Hint: BODMAS)
explanation: BODMAS stands for Brackets, Orders, Division, Multiplication, Addition, Subtraction. Restricted to the four operators listed in the options, that order is Division, Multiplication, Addition, Subtraction. In practice division and multiplication share one precedence level (as do addition and subtraction) and are evaluated left to right.

stem: What will be the output of the program?
explanation: The trailing semicolon on for(; i<=5; i++); makes the loop body empty, so the loop only increments i until the condition i <= 5 fails, which leaves i = 6. The printf is not part of the loop despite its indentation - indentation means nothing in C - so it executes once afterwards and prints 6, not 5 and not the sequence 0..5.

stem: A collection of interrelated records is called a
explanation: A database is by definition an organized collection of interrelated records or data. A spreadsheet is a tool for cells and formulas, a text file has no record structure, and an MIS is a system built on top of a database rather than the data collection itself.

stem: Unlike filters queries can be saved as in a database.
explanation: In a database (the wording follows MS Access terminology) a query is saved as a persistent object in the database and can be re-run later. A filter is transient: it applies only to the current view of the data and is discarded when you close it.

stem: The method of file organization in which data records in a file are arranged in a specified order according to key field is known as the
explanation: Sequential file organization stores records physically in ascending or descending order of a key field, so reading the file walks the keys in order. The trap is direct (random) access, which computes a record address by hashing or indexing and therefore imposes no ordering on the stored records.

stem: External database is
explanation: The stem is poorly worded, but of the four options only 'database created using a DBMS package' is actually a database. Word documents and Excel worksheets are file formats, not DBMS-managed databases, so 'All of the above' is also wrong.

stem: Data Manipulation Language (DML) is not to

options.label: A
options.text: Create information table in the Database

options.label: B
options.text: Insertion of new information into the Database

options.label: C
options.text: Deletion of information in the Database

options.label: D
options.text: Modification of information in the Database

explanation: DML covers manipulating the data itself: INSERT, DELETE, UPDATE and SELECT, which are options B, C and D. Creating a table is DDL (CREATE), not DML, so it is the one thing in the list DML is NOT used for. Note the negative stem: the correct answer is the odd one out.

stem: The minimal set of super key is called
explanation: A superkey is any attribute set that uniquely identifies a row; a candidate key is a superkey with no removable attribute, that is, a minimal superkey. The classic trap is 'primary key': the primary key is just the one candidate key the designer picks, so minimality defines candidate keys, not primary keys.

stem: A type of query that is placed within a WHERE or HAVING clause of another query is called
explanation: A query nested inside the WHERE or HAVING clause of another query is a subquery (nested query); the inner query is evaluated to supply values the outer query filters on. 'Super query', 'master query' and 'multi-query' are not SQL terms.

stem: A data dictionary doesn't provide information about:

options.label: B
options.text: The size of the disk storage device

explanation: A data dictionary stores metadata about the data: table and column names, data types, constraints, and where data is located. The physical capacity of the disk storage device is a hardware property, not metadata about the data, so it is the item the dictionary does not provide. Options C and D are garbled fragments that do not describe a data dictionary at all.

stem: In any hierarchy of data organization, the smallest entity to be processed as a single unit is called
explanation: The data hierarchy runs field, record, file, database, each level containing the one below it. A field (for example a single album title) is the smallest of these that is processed as one unit; a record is a group of fields, so it is larger, not smaller.

stem: A report generator is used to
explanation: A report generator takes data from the database and formats it for printed or on-screen output. Updating files is done by DML or a data entry form, and data entry is the operator's job, so neither is a report generator's function.

stem: Related fields in a data base are grouped to form
explanation: Related fields grouped together form one record (for example the title, artist and year fields of one album). Going one level up, related records grouped together form a data file, which is why option A is a level too high.

stem: In SQL, which command is used to remove a stored function from the database?
explanation: DROP is the SQL DDL verb for removing a schema object, so DROP FUNCTION removes a stored function. The trap is DELETE, which is DML and removes rows from a table; it cannot remove the function itself. REMOVE and ERASE are not SQL keywords.

stem: In SQL, which command is used to select only one copy of each set of duplicable rows
explanation: SELECT DISTINCT is the standard SQL clause that collapses duplicate rows so each distinct set of values is returned once. SELECT UNIQUE is only a legacy Oracle synonym and is not standard SQL, which is why 'All of the above' is wrong.

stem: In SQL, which command(s) is(are) used to enable/disable a database trigger?
explanation: ALTER TRIGGER trigger_name ENABLE / DISABLE is the command that turns an individual trigger on or off. ALTER TABLE can enable or disable all triggers on a table at once in some dialects, but it is not the command that targets a trigger, and MODIFY TRIGGER does not exist.

stem: Which of the following is not Modification of the Database?
explanation: Modifying a database means changing the stored data: insertion, deletion and updating. Sorting only changes the order in which rows are presented in a result set; the stored data is untouched, so it is not a modification.

stem: A command to remove a relation from an SQL database
explanation: DROP TABLE removes the relation itself - its rows, its structure and its definition in the schema. The classic trap is DELETE, which only removes rows and leaves the empty table in place; ALTER changes a table's structure but never removes it, and ERASE is not SQL at all.

stem: Which of the following in true regarding Referential Integrity?

options.label: A
options.text: Every primary-key value must match a primary-key value in an associated table

options.label: B
options.text: Every primary-key value must match a foreign-key value in an associated table

options.label: C
options.text: Every foreign-key value must match a primary-key value in an associated table

options.label: D
options.text: Every foreign-key value must match a foreign-key value in an associated table

explanation: Referential integrity constrains the CHILD table: every non-null foreign-key value must already exist as a primary-key value in the referenced parent table, so a foreign key can never point at a row that does not exist. The reversed option (every primary key must match a foreign key) is the trap - a parent row is perfectly legal with no children referencing it.

stem: In SQL, which command(s) is(are) used to redefine an index's future storage allocation
explanation: ALTER INDEX is the real SQL command for changing an existing index's properties, including its future storage allocation (e.g. ALTER INDEX ... STORAGE / REBUILD in Oracle). REDEFINE INDEX, MODIFY INDEX and DO INDEX are invented names and are not SQL commands.

stem: ____________ refers to the correctness and completeness of the data in a database?
explanation: Data integrity is defined as the correctness (accuracy, validity) and completeness of the data held in the database. The distractors are adjacent but distinct: security is about controlling who may access data, a constraint is one of the mechanisms used to enforce integrity, and data independence is about insulating applications from schema changes.

stem: Data Manipulation Language enables users to

options.label: A
options.text: Retrieval of information stored in database

options.label: B
options.text: Insertion of new information into the database

options.label: C
options.text: Deletion of information from the database

explanation: DML is the sub-language for working with the data itself: SELECT retrieves, INSERT adds, UPDATE changes and DELETE removes rows. Retrieval, insertion and deletion are all DML operations, so "All of the above" is correct; the trap is picking a single one and confusing DML with DDL (CREATE/ALTER/DROP), which acts on structure, not data.

stem: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of cloud computing?
explanation: The NIST essential characteristics are on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, and measured service. Fixed capacity is the direct opposite of rapid elasticity, so C is the odd one out; A, B and D are all on the list verbatim.

stem: The process of fetching an instruction, decoding it, and then executing it is known as the:
explanation: Fetch, decode, execute is the definition of the instruction cycle (also called the fetch-execute cycle). Memory cycle and bus cycle name the lower-level timing of a single memory or bus access, not the whole per-instruction sequence.

stem: Which technology enables multiple virtual machines on one physical server?
explanation: Virtualization inserts a hypervisor between hardware and guests, letting it multiplex one physical CPU, memory and NIC into many isolated virtual machines. Encryption, load balancing and firewalls are all security or traffic functions and create no VMs.

stem: Which of the following is a benefit of cloud computing?
explanation: Only C is stated as an advantage: the provider patches and upgrades the platform, so customers get updates automatically. A and B are worded as drawbacks (cloud lowers maintenance cost and gives near-unlimited scalability) and D is the manual work cloud backup services remove.

stem: In which model does the user have the least control over infrastructure?
explanation: Customer control decreases along IaaS > PaaS > SaaS. In IaaS you manage OS and runtime, in PaaS only the app and data, and in SaaS you consume a finished application and control nothing below it, so SaaS gives the least infrastructure control. Private Cloud is a deployment model, not a service model, so it does not belong on this scale.

stem: What is the role of a `bus` in a computer system?

options.label: C
options.text: To provide communication pathways between components

explanation: A bus is the shared set of address, data and control lines that carries signals between CPU, memory and I/O modules, so its role is communication. Storage is memory's job and execution is the CPU's job.

stem: Which company provides Azure cloud services?
explanation: Azure is Microsoft's cloud platform. Amazon sells AWS, Google sells GCP, IBM sells IBM Cloud.

stem: IAM stands for:
explanation: IAM = Identity and Access Management: the service that authenticates principals (users, roles, services) and authorizes what each may do via policies.

stem: Which technique protects data by converting it into unreadable form?
explanation: Encryption transforms plaintext into ciphertext that is unreadable without the key. Compression also makes data unreadable to the eye but is fully reversible by anyone with no secret, so it protects nothing; backup and replication just copy the data.

stem: SLA is an agreement between:
explanation: A Service Level Agreement is the contract between the cloud service provider and its customer, defining committed service levels (uptime, response time) and the remedies if they are missed.

stem: Serverless computing allows users to:
explanation: Serverless (FaaS) means the provider owns provisioning, scaling and patching of the servers; the customer only uploads code that runs on an event. Servers still exist, they are just invisible to the user, so A is the inverse of the model.

stem: AWS Lambda is an example of:
explanation: AWS Lambda runs event-triggered functions with no instance, OS or capacity to manage, which is the definition of serverless / Function as a Service. PaaS is the classic trap: it is close because the provider also manages the platform, but in PaaS you still deploy and keep a running application (and pay for its idle time), while Lambda bills only per invocation.

stem: Edge computing reduces:
explanation: Edge computing runs the processing at or near the data source instead of sending every request to a distant data centre, so the network round trip shrinks and latency drops. It does also cut backhaul bandwidth as a side effect, but latency is the reduction that follows directly from shortening the physical distance, and it is the defining benefit of the model.

stem: Which cloud feature automatically adjusts resources?
explanation: Elasticity is the cloud property of automatically scaling resources up when demand rises and back down when it falls, with no manual provisioning. The distractors are all real cloud features but none of them adjust capacity: virtualization is the underlying abstraction of hardware, encryption protects data, and metering only measures usage so it can be billed.

stem: What is a computer network?

options.label: B
options.text: A collection of interconnected devices that share resources and information.

options.label: C
options.text: A single powerful computer that manages all data.

options.label: D
options.text: A system for storing data on external hard drives.

explanation: A computer network is a set of interconnected devices (nodes) linked by transmission media so they can share resources and exchange information. Option A is the exact negation: 'standalone' computers are by definition not networked.

stem: Which of the following is NOT a basic terminology of computer networks?
explanation: Node, transmission media and networking devices are the basic building blocks used to define a network. The operating system is general computing software that runs on a host; it is not part of the vocabulary used to describe a network's structure, so it is the odd one out.

stem: What is the primary function of a Router in a network?

options.label: A
options.text: To connect devices within a single network segment.

options.label: B
options.text: To convert digital signals to analog signals.

options.label: C
options.text: To connect multiple networks and direct data packets to their correct destination.

options.label: D
options.text: To provide wireless connectivity to devices.

explanation: A router operates at the network layer: it connects separate networks and forwards packets toward their destination by consulting its routing table for the destination IP. Each distractor describes a different device - A is a switch or hub (one segment), B is a modem (digital-to-analog conversion), D is a wireless access point.

stem: Gmail is an example of:
explanation: Gmail is a finished application delivered over the browser, with Google managing the servers, runtime and the software itself, which is exactly Software as a Service. IaaS would give you raw VMs and PaaS would give you a runtime to deploy your own code; in both cases you still build the application, whereas with Gmail you only use it.

stem: Which type of network covers a small geographical area like a home or office?
explanation: Answer unverified: the source PDF gives no answer key for this item and no option is marked correct. On the facts the intended answer is C, LAN, since a Local Area Network is defined as covering a small area such as a home, office or single building. The ambiguity is D, PAN, which also fits 'home' loosely but is a much smaller personal range of a few metres around one person (Bluetooth, USB), so LAN remains the standard answer.

stem: What does TCP/IP stand for and what is its role?

options.label: A
options.text: Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol; breaks data into packets for secure and efficient delivery.

options.label: B
options.text: Total Control Protocol/Internal Protocol; manages internal network traffic.

options.label: C
options.text: Technical Communication Protocol/Internet Process; handles web browsing.

options.label: D
options.text: Transfer Control Program/Internet Provider; provides internet services.

explanation: TCP/IP is the Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol suite, the core protocol stack of the internet: IP handles addressing and routing of packets while TCP breaks data into segments, delivers them reliably and reassembles them in order. The other three options are invented expansions of the acronym.

stem: Which network device sends data to all devices connected to it, making it less efficient than a switch?
explanation: A hub is a physical-layer repeater with no addressing logic, so any frame arriving on one port is flooded out every other port, wasting bandwidth and creating a single collision domain. A switch is the efficient alternative because it learns MAC addresses and forwards each frame only to the destination port.

stem: Hybrid cloud is a combination of:
explanation: Hybrid cloud is a deployment model that combines a private cloud (or on-premise infrastructure) with a public cloud, letting an organisation keep sensitive workloads in-house while bursting to public capacity. The trap is options A and C, which mix up deployment models with service models: SaaS, PaaS and IaaS are service models and never combine to form a hybrid cloud.

stem: Multi-cloud refers to:
explanation: Multi-cloud means an organisation consumes services from more than one cloud provider, for example AWS plus Azure, typically to avoid vendor lock-in or improve resilience. Option A is the classic trap: using many services from a single provider is still single-cloud, no matter how many services are involved.

stem: Which is NOT a cloud service provider?
explanation: AWS, Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud are all cloud platforms that sell compute, storage and managed services. Oracle Database is a database product you install and license, not a cloud provider - the trap is the Oracle name, since Oracle's actual cloud offering is Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI), not 'Oracle Database'.

stem: What is the main characteristic of a Peer-to-Peer (P2P) network architecture?

options.label: A
options.text: It has a central server that manages all client nodes.

options.label: B
options.text: Each device is free to work as either a client or a server.

options.label: D
options.text: It is designed for wide geographical areas.

explanation: In a peer-to-peer architecture there is no dedicated server: every device can request a service (act as a client) and supply one (act as a server), which is what distinguishes it from client-server. Option A describes exactly the client-server model it is being contrasted with; P2P is defined by the role of the nodes, not by the medium (C) or the geographic span (D).

stem: The “pay-as-you-go” model means:
explanation: Pay-as-you-go bills strictly by actual consumption (compute hours, GB stored, requests made), so cost tracks usage and there is no charge for idle capacity. A fixed monthly payment (A) and a lifetime subscription (D) are the opposite arrangement: you pay the same regardless of what you use.

stem: Which of the following is a goal of computer networks?

options.label: A
options.text: To complicate computer usage for users.

options.label: C
options.text: To ensure security & protection of data and resources.

options.label: D
options.text: To decrease efficiency in hardware resource management.

explanation: Security and protection of data and resources is one of the standard stated goals of computer networking, alongside resource sharing, reliability and cost saving. The other three options are deliberately written as anti-goals (complicate usage, limit sharing, decrease efficiency), so they can be eliminated on sight.

stem: Which service model is best suited for application developers?
explanation: PaaS gives developers a ready runtime, middleware, database and deployment tooling, so they write and ship application code without provisioning or patching servers. IaaS would still leave them managing the OS and the stack themselves, and SaaS is a finished application with no room to develop anything.

stem: What is a PAN (Personal Area Network) primarily used for?

options.label: B
options.text: Connecting devices within a very short range, for individual use.

options.label: C
options.text: Providing internet access to a large organization.

options.label: D
options.text: Establishing secure, encrypted connections over public networks.

explanation: A PAN interconnects one person's own devices - phone, laptop, headset, smartwatch - over a very short range, typically a few metres via Bluetooth, infrared or USB. The distractors are the larger network classes: A describes a WAN or MAN, C describes a LAN or campus network, and D describes a VPN.

stem: What is Format Painter?

options.label: A
options.text: Copies the Format from a selected object or text & applies to the object you click

options.label: C
options.text: Saves the Format to a File

explanation: Format Painter copies the formatting (font, size, colour, borders, shading) of a selected object or text and applies that formatting to whatever you click next. It copies formatting only, never the content itself.

stem: What is Freeze Panes command in Excel?

options.label: B
options.text: Freezes the file you are working.

options.label: C
options.text: Closes the file you are working.

explanation: Freeze Panes locks the highlighted rows and columns in place so they stay visible while the rest of the sheet scrolls, which is how you keep headers on screen in a long table. It is the only option describing an area-based lock; it neither locks a file for editing nor closes it.

stem: For what Ctrl+C key board shortcut is used?
explanation: Ctrl+C copies the selection to the clipboard; Ctrl+X cuts and Ctrl+V pastes. Note the context: in a Unix terminal the same keystroke sends SIGINT to interrupt a running process, but this question is about the Office/Windows shortcut.

stem: How can the gridlines in an excel spreadsheet be invisible?

options.label: A
options.text: By unchecking the gridlines check button in options tab.

options.label: B
options.text: By clicking hide all toggle button in options tab.

explanation: Gridlines are hidden by clearing the gridlines checkbox in Excel's view/options settings, which turns off the on-screen display without altering any cell borders. There is no 'hide all' toggle button, so B describes a control that does not exist.

stem: What is Page Break Preview?

options.label: A
options.text: An editing view that displays your work sheet how it will print

options.label: B
options.text: An editing view to draw objects in a work sheet

options.label: C
options.text: An editing view to draw charts in excel

explanation: Page Break Preview is an editing view that shows the worksheet divided into printed pages, with the page breaks drawn as lines you can drag to change what lands on each page. It is still fully editable, it just previews the print layout; drawing objects and charts are unrelated features.

stem: Auto Filter command is under which Menu?
explanation: AutoFilter operates on a data list, so it sits on the Data menu (Data > Filter > AutoFilter in menu-era Excel; Data tab > Filter in the ribbon). Tools held options and macros, Insert held objects and functions.

stem: What Ctrl + P key board shortcut is for?

options.label: A
options.text: To paste an object you copied

explanation: Ctrl+P is the standard Print shortcut across Office and almost every Windows application. The distractors are other shortcuts: Ctrl+V pastes and Alt+F4 closes the application.

stem: For what Wrap Text check box is used?
explanation: Wrap Text (Format Cells > Alignment) breaks long text onto multiple lines inside the cell boundary and grows the row height to fit. It is a cell-level alignment setting and has nothing to do with page margins or the file.

stem: An Excel Spreadsheet can be inserted in a word document?
explanation: Yes. Insert > Object or Paste Special embeds or links a live Excel worksheet inside a Word document via OLE, so the sheet stays editable in place.

stem: Bullets and Numbering is under which menu?
explanation: Bullets and Numbering is paragraph formatting, so in classic Word menus it lives under Format (the option labelled C here). Modern ribbon Word moved it to Home > Paragraph.

stem: For what Ctrl + B keyboard shortcut is used?

options.label: B
options.text: To make the selected text & numbers bold.

explanation: Ctrl+B makes the selected text and numbers bold. Ctrl+U is the underline shortcut, which is the distractor in option C.

stem: Columns in a table can be deleted?
explanation: Yes. Word table columns can be removed with Table > Delete > Columns (or right-click the column and choose Delete Columns).

stem: What is Print Preview

options.label: A
options.text: Shows how a file looks, when it is printed

options.label: C
options.text: Shows, the table how it will print

explanation: Print Preview renders the whole file as it will appear on the printed page (pagination, margins, headers and footers) without sending anything to the printer. Option B describes the Print command itself, and option C wrongly narrows the preview to tables only.

stem: What is the function of Office Assistant?
explanation: The Office Assistant (Clippy) surfaced context-sensitive help topics and usage tips. It did not automate commands (that is what macros do) or save files automatically (that is AutoSave/AutoRecover).

stem: What is Word Art?

options.label: B
options.text: A command to create text effects.

options.label: C
options.text: A command to shade Text with colors.

explanation: WordArt inserts decorative text as a graphic object with effects such as curving, shadowing and 3-D. Shading text with color is a separate Highlight/Shading command, which is the trap in option C.

stem: For what, the subscript option is used?

options.label: A
options.text: To raise the text above baseline.

options.label: B
options.text: To lower the text below the baseline.

options.label: C
options.text: To draw line through the selected text.

explanation: Subscript lowers the text below the baseline, as in the 2 of H2O. Option A is the definition of superscript (raising above the baseline, as in x squared), the classic subscript/superscript swap.

stem: Sound can be added to a slide?
explanation: Yes. PowerPoint supports adding audio to a slide via Insert > Movies and Sounds (Insert > Audio in newer versions).

stem: Pictures can be added to a slide?
explanation: Yes. Images are added with Insert > Picture, either from a file or from clip art.

stem: Background of a slide can be changed?
explanation: Yes. Format > Background (Design > Format Background in newer versions) changes a slide's background color, gradient, texture or picture fill.

stem: An Organization chart can be drawn in Power Point?
explanation: Yes. PowerPoint ships an organization chart tool, reached via Insert > Diagram in older versions and Insert > SmartArt > Hierarchy in newer ones.

stem: For what is a Relationship window used in Access?

options.label: A
options.text: To define a relationship between tables & queries.

options.label: B
options.text: To define a relationship between reports.

options.label: C
options.text: To define a relationship between forms

explanation: The Relationships window defines joins between the objects that hold data, linking a primary key in one table to a foreign key in another (queries can also be placed there). Forms and reports only display data, so they have no relationships to define, which rules out B and C.

stem: Forms are used to display and enter the data?
explanation: Yes. Forms are the Access user interface bound to a table or query, used both to view existing records and to key in new ones.

stem: Forms are created based on the data from Reports?
explanation: No, the direction is backwards. A form's record source is a table or a query; reports are output-only consumers of data and can never act as a data source for a form.

stem: For what Queries are used in Access?
explanation: Queries retrieve, filter and join data held in tables (or in other queries). Forms and reports consume query output rather than store data, so they are not things you query.

stem: Reports are created in Design View?
explanation: Yes. Design View is the authoring surface where a report's layout, sections and bound controls are built and edited. A wizard or AutoReport can generate a first draft, but it hands the result to Design View for actual design work.

stem: Storage which stores or retains data after power off is called-
explanation: Retaining data after power is removed is the definition of non-volatile storage (ROM, hard disk, flash). Volatile storage (RAM) loses its contents when power is cut. Sequential and direct describe how storage is accessed, not whether it survives power off, so they answer a different question.

stem: A permanent memory, which halls data and instruction for start-up the computer and does not erase data after power off.
explanation: The start-up (bootstrap/BIOS) instructions must be present the instant power comes on, before anything can be loaded from disk, so they live in ROM, which is non-volatile and permanent. RAM is the trap: it is memory, but it is volatile and empty at power-on.

stem: Which of the following memories must be refreshed many times per second?
explanation: Dynamic RAM stores each bit as charge on a tiny capacitor, and that charge leaks away, so every cell must be read and rewritten (refreshed) thousands of times per second. Static RAM holds bits in a flip-flop and needs no refresh; ROM and EPROM are non-volatile and hold their contents with no power at all.

stem: USB-type storage device is -
explanation: A USB flash drive holds data outside the CPU's directly addressable main memory and persists it, which makes it secondary (auxiliary) storage. Primary is the trap: primary/main memory means RAM, which the CPU reads directly. 'Axillary' is a misspelling of auxiliary and is not the term the key uses.

stem: Which of the following places the common data elements in order from smallest to largest?

options.label: A
options.text: Character, File, Record, Field, Database, File

options.label: D
options.text: Bit, Byte, Character, Record, Field, File, Database

explanation: The standard data hierarchy builds upward: character (a single symbol) -> field (a group of characters, e.g. a name) -> record (a group of related fields) -> file (a group of records) -> database (a group of files). Option D is the trap: it starts correctly with bit and byte but then puts Record before Field, which inverts the containment.

stem: Which device is used to back up the data?
explanation: Backup only requires a medium that can hold a second copy of the data, and floppy disks, magnetic tape and a network drive all qualify, so 'All of the above' is correct. Tape is the traditional answer and is the trap if you pick a single option; the question asks which device can be used, not which is best.

stem: A half byte is known as_____.
explanation: A byte is 8 bits, so half a byte is 4 bits, which is called a nibble. Option C ('half byte') is a restatement of the stem rather than the technical name.

stem: Main memory of computer is -
explanation: Main memory (RAM plus ROM) sits on the motherboard and is directly addressable by the CPU, which makes it internal or primary memory. External/auxiliary refers to secondary storage such as disks and USB drives, which the CPU cannot address directly and must load into main memory first.

stem: What is the permanent memory built into your computer called?
explanation: Permanent memory built into the machine and readable at power-on is ROM. RAM is volatile so it cannot be permanent, and CD-ROM is the trap: it is read-only but it is removable optical media, not memory built into the computer.

stem: With a CD you can...........
explanation: The bank treats a plain CD (CD-ROM) as a read-only medium: it is pressed at manufacture and a normal drive can only read it. Note the item is loose, because CD-R and CD-RW discs plus a burner are writable, so 'read and write' is defensible in the real world; on the exam, CD alone means read.

stem: RAM is a.......memory-
explanation: RAM is the working store the CPU reads and writes directly while programs run, which is by definition the main (primary) memory. 'Internal' is the trap here because it is also true of RAM, but it is a location, not the standard name for the memory tier; auxiliary/external means secondary storage such as disk.

stem: __________ is the permanent memory built into your computer called.
explanation: Permanent, non-volatile memory built into the computer is ROM, which holds the firmware that runs at power-on. RAM is volatile and loses its contents; DVD-ROM is removable media, not built-in memory.

stem: Magnetic tape is not practical for applications where data must be quickly recalled because tape is __________.
explanation: Tape is a sequential-access medium: to reach a record the drive must physically wind past everything stored before it, so access time depends on position and can be minutes. A disk is random access and can seek straight to the data, which is why tape is used for backup and archive, not for data that must be recalled quickly.

stem: What is the main advantage of magnetic core memory over semiconductor RAM memory?

options.label: C
options.text: A bit does not have to write after reading

explanation: Magnetic core memory stores each bit as the magnetisation direction of a ferrite ring, which persists with no power, so it is non-volatile, unlike semiconductor RAM. The other options are wrong in the opposite direction: core is bulkier, more expensive, and its reads are destructive, so a bit does have to be written back after being read.

stem: Hard disc drives are considered.........storage-
explanation: A hard disk stores bits as magnetised regions on a platter, and magnetisation survives power off, so an HDD is non-volatile storage. 'Flash' is the trap: flash is also non-volatile, but it is the semiconductor technology used in SSDs and USB sticks, not in a magnetic hard disc drive.

stem: Permanent instructions that the computer uses when it is turned on and that cannot be changed by other instructions are contained in-
explanation: The bootstrap/BIOS instructions that run at power-on and cannot be overwritten by ordinary programs are stored in ROM, which is non-volatile and read-only. RAM and DRAM are volatile and empty at power-on; the ALU and CPU execute instructions rather than store them permanently.

stem: Flash memory is also called as __________.
explanation: The bank's intended answer is Flash RAM, the informal name used because flash can be read and written like RAM while still being non-volatile. Be aware the item is technically shaky: flash is an EEPROM variant, so 'flash ROM' is the more accurate label and is the distractor most likely to tempt you; go with the bank's Flash RAM on this exam.

stem: A Winchester disk is a_________.
explanation: Legacy term: a Winchester disk is the sealed fixed hard-disk assembly with several rigid platters mounted on one spindle, i.e. a disk stack. The distractors describe the opposite properties: it is not removable (the platters are sealed in with the heads) and not flexible (that is a floppy).

stem: Typical acronym of reusable optical storage will be......
explanation: CD-RW expands to CD-ReWritable, the optical format whose recording layer can be erased and rewritten, so it is the reusable one. CD-R/CD-ROM are write-once or read-only, DVD names a capacity family rather than a reusability class, and RPM is a rotation speed unit, not a medium.

stem: Data gathering in computer means, they allow to use.........data.
explanation: Answer unverified: the source key marks D (Store) but the review panel derived B (Input), and the two disagree. The stem is a garbled version of the standard input-process-output-storage item: 'gathering' data is the intake step (input), while storing is a separate function listed elsewhere in the same series. Treat this item as ambiguous and learn the four functions rather than the key.

stem: When we work on any document on PC, it is stored temporarily on-
explanation: An open document is held in RAM, the volatile working memory the CPU reads and writes while you edit; it only becomes permanent when you save it to disk. ROM is read-only and cannot hold your edits, and the CPU itself only holds a few words in registers, not a document.

stem: The faster, costlier and relatively small from of storage managed by computer system hardware is:
explanation: In the memory hierarchy, cache is the level that is fastest, most expensive per byte, smallest, and managed by hardware rather than by the OS. Main memory and disk are larger, slower, cheaper per byte, and their allocation is managed by the operating system, which is the discriminator the stem is testing with 'managed by computer system hardware'.

stem: The analytical engine developed during first generation of computers used __________ as a memory unit.
explanation: Babbage's Analytical Engine held its numbers in the 'store', built from columns of geared counter wheels, with each wheel position representing one decimal digit. Cards are the classic trap: punched cards fed the Engine its program and input, but they were not the memory unit.

stem: Which of the following is used to hold ROM, RAM, CPU and expansion cards?
explanation: The motherboard is the main printed circuit board that physically carries the CPU socket, RAM slots, ROM/BIOS chip, and the expansion slots for add-in cards. The bus is only the set of wires that carries signals between those components, not the board that holds them.

stem: Which of the following medium is used between CPU & RAM TO speed up the processing power of a CPU?
explanation: Cache memory sits between the CPU and main memory, holding recently and frequently used data in faster SRAM so the CPU stalls less often on slow RAM accesses. Virtual memory is the trap: it works in the opposite direction, extending RAM onto the much slower disk, so it adds latency rather than removing it.

stem: Breaking logical memory into blocks of the same size is called as:
explanation: In paging, the logical (virtual) address space is divided into equal-size blocks called pages, and physical memory is divided into same-size blocks called frames. Frames is the deliberate trap: the two terms are the same size and are used as a pair, so remember logical to pages, physical to frames.

stem: Which one is random access memory-
explanation: RAM is by definition random-access memory: any location can be read or written in the same time regardless of address. ROM and PROM are also randomly addressable but are read-only, which is why 'All of these' is the intended trap and RAM is the keyed answer.

stem: Where, data will remain intact even when the computer is turned of
explanation: Secondary storage (hard disk, SSD, optical, tape) is non-volatile, so its contents survive a power-off. RAM, which is primary storage, is volatile and loses its contents when power is removed, which is why both A and D are wrong.

stem: For permanent memory in computer objects used are -
explanation: Floppy disc, magnetic tape, hard disc, and optical disc are all non-volatile media that retain data without power, so every listed option qualifies as 'permanent' memory and 'All of these' is correct. The trap is picking a single familiar medium such as hard disc when the category is what is being tested.

stem: Main memory works in conjunction with __________.
explanation: Main memory works directly with the CPU: the processor fetches instructions and data from it over the system bus on every cycle. RAM is the trap here, because main memory IS RAM, so choosing A makes the statement circular rather than identifying the component it partners with.

stem: SRAM stands for-
explanation: SRAM stands for Static Random-Access Memory. 'Static' means each bit is held in a flip-flop and needs no periodic refresh, unlike DRAM whose capacitors must be recharged, which is why SRAM is faster and is used for cache.

stem: Built in memory of computer is.................
explanation: ROM is the built-in, non-volatile memory written at manufacture that holds the firmware/BIOS the machine needs to start up. RAM is wrong because it is volatile and empty at power-on, and PROM/EPROM are user-programmable variants rather than the memory that ships built into the machine.

stem: Virtual memory is_______.

options.label: A
options.text: memory on the hard disk that the CPU uses an extended RAM

options.label: C
options.text: only necessary if you do not have any RAM in your computer

options.label: D
options.text: a backup device for floppy disk

explanation: Virtual memory reserves an area of the hard disk (the page or swap file) and lets the OS move pages between it and RAM, so programs can address more memory than is physically installed. Option B is the trap: the whole point of virtual memory is that the backing store is on disk, not in RAM.

stem: USB stands for-
explanation: USB stands for Universal Serial Bus. It is 'serial' because data is sent one bit at a time over a differential pair, and 'universal' because a single connector standard replaced the older keyboard, mouse, serial, and parallel ports.

stem: In a typical memory experiment, participants are asked to recall stimuli in which phase?
explanation: Off-syllabus psychology trivia: a memory experiment has a presentation (study) phase where stimuli are shown and a test phase where retrieval is measured, so recall is elicited in the test phase. 'Material' and 'distinction' are not phase names at all.

stem: Participants are NOT told that they will be tested on a list of words presented in an experiment. This provides evidence of
explanation: Incidental learning is learning that happens without any intent to learn, so not warning participants about the later test is the defining setup. Intentional learning is the opposite condition (participants are told they will be tested).

stem: A test where participants are asked to retrieve in any order stimuli previously presented is
explanation: Free recall means producing the studied items in any order with no cues provided. Cued recall would supply a hint at test, and implicit/explicit memory describe the type of memory, not the test format.

stem: Participants take part in an experiment where they learn a number of words and are told that they will be tested later on what they have learned. This is a test of
explanation: Telling participants they will be tested and then asking them to deliberately retrieve the list makes it a conscious, intentional retrieval, which is the definition of an explicit memory test. Recognition is a trap: it is one specific explicit test format, but the stem never says the words are re-presented for a yes/no judgement.

stem: Storage is

options.label: A
options.text: the stage of memory where information is interpreted and transferred

options.label: B
options.text: the stage of memory where information is committed

options.label: C
options.text: the stage of memory most affected by direct manipulation in experiments

options.label: D
options.text: the stage of memory between presentation of stimuli and before test

explanation: Memory has three stages: encoding (interpreting and committing information), storage (retaining it), and retrieval (getting it back). Storage is therefore the retention interval between presentation of the stimuli and the test; options A and B describe encoding.

stem: Investigating what factors can affect memory storage can be done by

options.label: A
options.text: manipulating what happens before the test phase

options.label: B
options.text: informing people that they will be tested

options.label: D
options.text: manipulating what happens at the presentation phase

explanation: To isolate storage you must vary only what happens during the retention interval, that is, after presentation and before the test. Manipulating the presentation phase (D) targets encoding, and providing recall cues (C) targets retrieval.

stem: Which is NOT typically used to test memory retrieval?
explanation: The dot probe task measures attentional bias toward emotional stimuli; it is not a memory test at all. Word stem completion (implicit), cued recall (explicit) and serial position effects are all standard retrieval measures.

stem: With immediate testing, recall accuracy is poorest for items that occur in which position of a list?
explanation: The serial position curve is U-shaped: early items benefit from primacy (rehearsal into long-term memory) and late items from recency (still in short-term store), so middle items get neither boost and are recalled worst.

stem: Immediately testing recall can lead to greater recall for words at the end of a list. This is
explanation: Better recall of the last items on an immediate test is the recency effect. Serial position effect is the trap: it is the umbrella term for the whole U-shaped curve (primacy plus recency), not the end-of-list advantage specifically.

stem: The recency effect in memory retrieval can be reduced when

options.label: B
options.text: short- to long-term memory interference is introduced

options.label: C
options.text: a backwards masking task is used

explanation: Recency depends on the final items still sitting in the short-term store, so lengthening the retention interval (for example by inserting a filled delay before the test) wipes it out. Preventing rehearsal mainly attacks primacy, not recency.

stem: Which authors proposed the modal model of memory?
explanation: The modal (multi-store) model of sensory store to short-term store to long-term store is Atkinson and Shiffrin (1968). Craik and Lockhart (1972) proposed levels of processing, and Tulving and Thomson proposed encoding specificity.

stem: The modal model of memory was challenged on the grounds that

options.label: A
options.text: impaired short-term memory does not disrupt long-term memory

options.label: B
options.text: Short-term memory has a limited capacity

options.label: C
options.text: longer time in short-term memory predicts likelihood of long-term memory

options.label: D
options.text: long-term memory has a limited capacity

explanation: The modal model routes everything through short-term memory into long-term memory, so it predicts that damaging short-term memory should block new long-term learning. Neuropsychological cases with badly impaired short-term memory but intact long-term learning break that serial chain. Options B and D are not challenges: limited short-term capacity is a claim the model itself makes.

stem: According to levels of processing, which of the following leads to the deepest level of memory?
explanation: In Craik and Lockhart's levels of processing, depth increases from structural to phonological to semantic, so processing meaning produces the most durable trace. Perceptual processing is the shallowest level.

stem: A critical issue with the ‘levels of processing’ account of memory is

options.label: A
options.text: the distinction between perceptual and semantic processing

options.label: B
options.text: it is not compatible with the modal model

options.label: D
options.text: it does not explain how information is retrieved

explanation: The standard criticism is circularity: depth of processing is defined by how well the material is later remembered, and there is no independent measure of depth, so the theory cannot be falsified.

stem: Retrieval cues can improve recall because of
explanation: A cue helps only to the extent that the information it carries was part of the trace laid down at encoding, that is, overlap between the cue and what was encoded (encoding specificity). Interference and decay explain forgetting, not why cues help.

stem: ‘Specific operations […] determine what is stored, and what is stored determines what retrieval cues are effective’ refers to what term?
explanation: That sentence is Tulving and Thomson's statement of the encoding specificity principle: retrieval succeeds when the cue matches what was actually stored at encoding. Context-dependent memory is a consequence of this principle, not the principle itself.

stem: Retroactive inhibition is a term of memory
explanation: Retroactive inhibition is new learning interfering with the retrieval of older material, so it is an interference account of forgetting, not a decay or delay account (decay says traces fade with time on their own).

stem: Having prior memory associations that make it difficult to form new memory associations is termed
explanation: Proactive inhibition is old learning interfering forward in time with new learning. Retroactive is the mirror image (new interferes with old), which is the trap here.

stem: Which of the following was proposed by Keppel (1968) to be most likely?

options.label: A
options.text: prior learning may interfere with new learning only when items are similar, but new learning interferes with all old learning

options.label: B
options.text: old learning can interfere with new learning, but new learning interferes with all old learning

options.label: C
options.text: all forgetting is caused by non-specific proactive inhibition

options.label: D
options.text: old learning only interferes with new learning when encoded in the same modality

explanation: Keppel's asymmetry: proactive interference is restricted, old learning only impairs new learning when the material is similar, whereas retroactive interference is general, new learning impairs old learning broadly. Option B is a near-duplicate that drops the similarity restriction, which is exactly the discriminating detail.

stem: What explains the mathematical forgetting curve?
explanation: Jost's law is the formal statement that of two equally strong memories the older one decays more slowly, which is what gives the forgetting curve its decelerating (roughly power/logarithmic) shape. The distractors are empirical effects, not accounts of the curve's mathematical form.

stem: Lansdale and Baguley (2008) predict that the probability of correct recall depends on
explanation: Lansdale and Baguley's fragmentation account treats a memory as a set of traces, so the probability of correct recall is a function of the proportion of correct traces available at test. The other options name principles about cues and interference rather than a quantity that predicts recall probability.

stem: The study of how the ‘War of the Ghosts’ story accounts became altered at recall was carried out by
explanation: The 'War of the Ghosts' study is Frederic Bartlett (1932), Remembering, the classic demonstration that recall is reconstructive and shaped by schemas. Godden and Baddeley (1975) is the diver study on context-dependent memory, a common distractor here.

stem: Eyewitness testimonies are an example of what kind of memory experiment?
explanation: Witnesses do not know in advance that they will be tested, so they never intend to memorise the event. Learning without intent to learn is incidental learning; intentional learning (A) is the trap, since it describes a lab participant deliberately told to memorise a list.

stem: Which key academic is involved in the study of memory as a reconstructive process?
explanation: Elizabeth Loftus is the name attached to reconstructive memory and the misinformation effect (leading questions altering what witnesses 'remember'). Baddeley and Hitch are the working-memory model, not reconstruction.

stem: The accuracy of eyewitness testimonies can be improved with
explanation: The cognitive interview (Fisher and Geiselman) is the standard evidence-based technique for improving eyewitness accuracy: report everything, reinstate context, change order, change perspective. Recovered memories (A) are the opposite, they are associated with false memory, not accuracy.

stem: What can help improve the accuracy of eye witness testimonies?
explanation: Context reinstatement, mentally putting yourself back at the scene, is a core component of the cognitive interview and reliably boosts recall by restoring retrieval cues. Mnemonics and distributed practice are study techniques for deliberate learning, not tools for recovering an unrehearsed witnessed event.

stem: Memory can be improved when information to be learnt is
explanation: Organising material (hierarchies, categories) gives recall a retrieval structure and reliably improves memory. Note the item is weak: elaboration (D) is also a genuine memory booster, so both A and D are defensible; the key is A.

stem: Which is a good method of revision:
explanation: Spaced (distributed) learning beats massed cramming: the same total study time split across separated sessions produces markedly better long-term retention. Intense single sessions and late-night cramming are the classic wrong answers.

stem: How many address bits are needed to select all memory locations in the 2118 16K × 1 RAM?
explanation: 16K = 16 x 1024 = 16384 locations, and 16384 = 2^14, so 14 address bits are needed. The trap is 16, which comes from reading the '16' in '16K' rather than computing the power of two.

stem: The check sum method of testing a ROM:

options.label: A
options.text: indicates if the data in more than one memory location is incorrect.

options.label: B
options.text: provides a means for locating and correcting data errors in specific memory locations.

options.label: C
options.text: allows data errors to be pinpointed to a specific memory location.

options.label: D
options.text: simply indicates that the contents of the ROM are incorrect.

explanation: A checksum sums the ROM's contents and compares the total against a stored expected value, so a mismatch tells you only that something in the ROM is wrong. It cannot say which location failed and cannot correct it, and it can even miss errors that cancel out in the sum.

stem: Refer to the given figures (a) and (b). A logic analyzer is used to check the circuit in figure (a) and displays the waveforms shown in figure (b). The actual analyzer display shows all four data outputs, Q0-Q3. The analyzer's cursor is placed at position X and all four of the data output lines show a Low-level output. What is wrong, if anything, with the circuit?

options.label: A
options.text: Nothing is wrong, according to the display. The outputs are in the open state and should show zero output voltage.

options.label: B
options.text: The circuit is in the READ mode and the outputs, Q0-Q3, should reflect the contents of the memory at that address. The chip is defective; replace the chip.

options.label: C
options.text: The circuit is in the      mode and should be writing the contents of the selected address to Q0–Q3.

options.label: D
