options.text: The Q0–Q3 lines can be either LOW or HIGH, since the chip is in the tristate mode in which case their level is unpredictable.

explanation: At cursor X the timing diagram has the RAM in write mode with its three-state outputs disabled, so Q0-Q3 are in the open (high-impedance) state and the analyzer reads them as zero volts, i.e. LOW. Nothing is broken. Choice B is the trap: it assumes the chip is in READ mode, which the control lines at X do not show.

stem: What is the meaning of RAM, and what is its primary role?

options.label: A
options.text: Readily Available Memory; it is the first level of memory used by the computer in all of its operations.

options.label: B
options.text: Random Access Memory; it is memory that can be reached by any sub-system within a computer, and at any time.

options.label: C
options.text: Random Access Memory; it is the memory used for short-term temporary data storage within the computer.

options.label: D
options.text: Resettable Automatic Memory; it is memory that can be used and then automatically reset, or cleared, after being read from or written to.

explanation: RAM is Random Access Memory: volatile read/write memory used as the computer's short-term working store for the code and data currently in use. B is the trap, 'random access' refers to the ability to reach any address in constant time, not to which sub-systems may access it.

stem: The storage element for a static RAM is the ________.
explanation: A static RAM cell stores its bit in a latch (flip-flop), which holds state as long as power is applied and needs no refresh. The capacitor is the DRAM storage element, and its charge leaks away, which is exactly why DRAM must be refreshed.

stem: In a DRAM, what is the state of R/W during a read operation?
explanation: The line is R/W-bar: HIGH selects read, LOW selects write (the bar applies to the write half). So during a read it is HIGH. LOW is the trap for anyone who forgets the active-low convention on the W.

stem: The condition occurring when two or more devices try to write data to a bus simultaneously is called ________.
explanation: Two or more devices driving the same bus lines at the same time is bus contention; it is prevented by three-state outputs and address decoding that enable only one driver at a time. Address decoding and multiplexing are the mechanisms that avoid the problem, not names for it.

stem: For the given circuit, what memory location is being addressed?
explanation: The address lines in the figure give A7..A0 = 11111001. Decoded as row/column: rows from A4-A0 = 11001 = 25 and columns from A7-A5 = 111 = 7, so the location is 7 x 32 + 25 = 249, which is just binary 11111001 read as a decimal number.

stem: A command that takes what has been typed into the computer and can be seen on the screen and sends it to the printer for output on paper______.
explanation: Straight definition item: the print command sends what is on screen or typed to the printer for output on paper. No reasoning behind it.

stem: RAM stands for :
explanation: RAM stands for Random Access Memory, meaning any address can be reached in roughly the same time regardless of its position. 'Read Access Memory' is the invented distractor.

stem: _______ tags, when placed on an animal, can be used to record and track in a database all of the animal’s movements.
explanation: RFID (Radio Frequency Identification) tags carry a chip and antenna that a reader energises by radio, so each time the tagged animal passes a reader its ID and timestamp are logged to a database. GPS is a positioning system, not a tag read by a scanner, and POS/PPS are not tags at all.

stem: Chip is a common nickname for a (n):
explanation: Answer unverified: the source PDF marks no key for this item. 'Chip' is the everyday nickname for an integrated circuit, a silicon die packing many transistors, so C is the intended answer. Transistor and resistor are individual components on the chip, and semiconductor is the material (silicon) the chip is made from, which is the classic trap here.

stem: All of the following are examples of storage devices except:
explanation: Storage devices retain data (hard disk, USB flash drive, DVD drive), while a printer only produces output on paper and holds nothing for later retrieval, so the printer is the exception.

stem: ____ is any part of the computer that you can physically touch.
explanation: Hardware is by definition every physical, touchable part of the computer. Device and peripheral are narrower subsets (a peripheral is only the external hardware), and an application is software, which has no physical form.

stem: A keyboard is this kind of device—
explanation: A keyboard sends keystrokes into the computer, so it is an input device. Direction of data flow is the whole test: into the machine is input, out of it (monitor, printer, speaker) is output.

stem: The ______, also called the “brains” of the computer, is responsible for processing data.
explanation: The CPU executes instructions and performs arithmetic and logic, which is why it is called the brains of the computer. The motherboard is only the board it sits on, and RAM/memory just holds the data and instructions the CPU works on.

stem: During the boot process, the ____________ looks for the system files.
explanation: On power-up the BIOS firmware runs POST and then searches the boot devices in order for the boot sector and system files, handing control to the OS loader. The CPU executes the BIOS code but is not the thing doing the searching, and a CD or DVD is just one place it may look.

stem: _______ is an output device that lets you see what the computer is doing.
explanation: The monitor is the output device that continuously displays what the computer is doing. The printer is also output but produces a static paper copy rather than a live view, which is the distractor to reject.

stem: Which of the following is an example of an input device?
explanation: A scanner converts a paper image into digital data and feeds it into the computer, so it is an input device. Speaker and printer are outputs, and a CD is storage media, not a device.

stem: The barcode which is used on all types of items, is read by a scanning device directly into the computer. What is the name of this scanning device?
explanation: Retail barcodes are read by a laser scanner, which sweeps a laser beam across the bars and measures the reflected light. A wand must be dragged across the code by hand, OCR reads printed characters rather than bars, and MICR reads magnetic ink on cheques.

stem: A computer’s hard disk is
explanation: A hard disk is a physical component you can touch, so it is hardware. It stores the operating system and software, but it is not itself either of those, which is the trap in options B and C.

stem: Surgeons can perform delicate operations by manipulating devices through computers instead of manually. This technology is known as:
explanation: Operating through computer-controlled instruments instead of by hand is robotics (robot-assisted surgery). Simulation would mean practising on a model rather than a real patient, and forensics/forecasting are unrelated.

stem: The piece of hardware that converts your computer’s digital signal to an analog signal that can travel over telephone lines is called
explanation: Answer unverified: the source PDF marks no key, but D (Modem) is the only real answer. Modem is short for modulator-demodulator: it modulates the computer's digital bits onto an analog carrier for the phone line and demodulates the incoming analog signal back to digital. The other three options are nonsense distractors.

stem: Computers that are portable and convenient for users who travel are known as _____
explanation: Laptops are the battery-powered, portable computers carried by travellers. Supercomputers, minicomputers and file servers are all stationary machines that stay in a data centre or office.

stem: The resolution of a monitor is measured in
explanation: Of the four options only dots (pixels) per inch measures resolution, i.e. how finely detail is packed onto the screen. Hz is the refresh rate and diagonal inches is the physical screen size, neither of which is resolution. Note the wording is loose: real monitor resolution is normally quoted as a pixel count such as 1920x1080, and DPI is more properly a print/pixel-density measure.

stem: CD-ROM is a kind of
explanation: Answer unverified: the source PDF marks no key, but A (Optical disk) is correct. A CD-ROM stores data as pits and lands read by a laser beam, which is the definition of optical media. Magnetic disks (hard disks, floppies) store data as magnetised regions, and magneto-optical drives combine both, using a laser to heat the medium before a magnetic write.

stem: BIOS stands for :
explanation: BIOS stands for Basic Input/Output System, the firmware that initialises hardware at power-on and provides the low-level input/output routines the OS loader relies on.

stem: The CPU and memory are located on the:
explanation: The motherboard is the main circuit board that carries the CPU socket, the RAM slots and the buses connecting them. Expansion boards plug into it for extra functions, and storage/output devices are separate peripherals attached to it.

stem: LCD stands for :
explanation: LCD stands for Liquid Crystal Display: a backlit panel whose liquid crystal cells twist to block or pass light per pixel.

stem: Which of the following is used to indicate the location on the computer monitor
explanation: The cursor is the on-screen marker showing the current position (the pointer, or the insertion point in text). The mouse is the input device that moves the cursor, so it is the position indicator's cause, not the indicator itself, which is the trap in option A.

stem: First generation computer systems used
explanation: First generation computers (roughly 1940-1956, e.g. ENIAC, UNIVAC) were built from vacuum tubes. Transistors are the classic trap here: they define the second generation, which is why 'Both' is wrong.

stem: Such types of printers, in which the printing head contacts with the paper in printing process, are called as :
explanation: An impact printer is defined by the print head physically striking an inked ribbon against the paper (dot-matrix, daisy-wheel, line printers). Laser is the trap: it is a non-impact printer, since it fuses toner without any mechanical contact.

stem: The term that we use to describe physical components of a computer
explanation: Hardware is the term for the physical, tangible components of a computer. Software is the intangible instructions, and an operating system is one kind of software, so both are wrong.

stem: Which part of the computer helps to store information ?
explanation: The disk drive is the only storage device in the list. Keyboard is input, monitor and printer are output; none of them retain data.

stem: QWERTY is used with reference to
explanation: QWERTY names the standard keyboard layout, taken from the first six letters on the top letter row.

stem: DVD stands for :
explanation: DVD officially stands for Digital Versatile Disc. 'Digital Video Disc' is the trap: it was the early informal name, dropped once the format was used for data and audio as well as video.

stem: Which of the following is not an input device?
explanation: The monitor displays results to the user, so it is an output device. Keyboard, joystick and microphone all feed data into the computer.

stem: This component is required to process data into information and consists of integrated circuits.
explanation: The CPU is the integrated-circuit chip that executes instructions and turns raw data into information. RAM and the hard disk only store data, and ROM only holds fixed firmware; none of them process it.

stem: What is object of UPS?

options.label: B
options.text: To increase the speed of a computer

explanation: A UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supply) holds a battery that supplies backup power when mains power fails, giving time to save work and shut down cleanly. It does nothing for storage capacity or speed, so 'All of the above' is wrong.

stem: Which of the following groups consist of only output devices ?
explanation: Plotter, printer and monitor are all output devices. Every other group is spoiled by one input device: scanner in A, keyboard in B, mouse in C.

stem: Information travels between components on the mother board 1 through:
explanation: A bus is the set of parallel wires on the motherboard that carries data, addresses and control signals between components. CMOS is a small memory holding BIOS settings and bays are physical slots for drives, so neither transports information.

stem: A framwork in which circuit boards can be mounted is…
explanation: A card cage is the metal frame or chassis with guide rails and a backplane into which circuit boards are slotted and mounted.

stem: What type of device is a Computer Printer ?
explanation: A printer takes data from the computer and renders it on paper for the user, which makes it an output device.

stem: All of the following are examples of input devices EXCEPT a:
explanation: The question asks for the exception. Scanner, mouse and keyboard all send data into the computer; the printer only receives data from it, so the printer is the output device and the correct answer.

stem: A Plastic card similar to a credit card but having some memory and a microprocessor embedded within it is
explanation: A chip card (smart card) is a credit-card-sized plastic card with an embedded microprocessor and memory. Punched tape, card punch and magnetic tape are passive legacy media with no processor.

stem: Physical structure of computer is called:
explanation: The physical structure of a computer, the parts you can touch, is its hardware. Software is the programs, and humanware refers to the people who operate the system.

stem: When you quickly press and release the left mouse button twice , you are—
explanation: Two quick presses and releases of the left (primary) button is a double-click. A single press is primary-clicking, and a right-button press is secondary-clicking.

stem: The following are all computing devices , except ………
explanation: Notebooks, cellular phones and PDAs each have their own processor and run programs, so they are computing devices. A digital scanner is only a peripheral input device attached to a computer, so it is the exception.

stem: The main virtue for using single Bus structure is ____________

options.label: C
options.text: Cost effective connectivity and ease of attaching peripheral devices

explanation: By using a single BUS structure we can minimize the amount of hardware (wire) required and thereby reducing the cost.

stem: ______ are used to overcome the difference in data transfer speeds of various devices.
explanation: By using Buffer registers, the processor sends the data to the I/O device at the processor speed and the data gets stored in the buffer. After that the data gets sent to or from the buffer to the devices at the device speed.

stem: To extend the connectivity of the processor bus we use ________
explanation: PCI BUS is used to connect other peripheral devices that require a direct connection with the processor.

stem: IBM developed a bus standard for their line of computers ‘PC AT’ called _____
explanation: ISA stands for Industry Standard Architecture, the 16-bit expansion bus IBM introduced with the PC/AT in 1984. Legacy trivia: the other three option names are not real bus standards.

stem: The bus used to connect the monitor to the CPU is ______
explanation: Answer unverified: the source PDF prints no key for this item, and the widely copied textbook key is 'SCSI bus', which is factually wrong (SCSI is a storage and scanner peripheral bus and has never carried video). The defensible answer is PCI: a monitor is driven by a display adapter card, and that card sits on the PCI/AGP expansion bus, so the path is monitor -> graphics card -> PCI -> CPU. Memory bus and Rambus both serve DRAM, not display output.

stem: _____ register Connected to the Processor bus is a single-way transfer capable.
explanation: The Z register is a special register which can interact with the processor BUS only.

stem: In multiple Bus organisation, the registers are collectively placed and referred as ______
explanation: In a multiple-bus organisation the general-purpose registers are grouped into one multi-ported block, read from and written to over separate buses in the same cycle. That block is called the register file. 'Register block' and 'set registers' are not standard terms.

stem: The main advantage of multiple bus organisation over a single bus is _____

options.label: A
options.text: Reduction in the number of cycles for execution

options.label: B
options.text: Increase in size of the registers

explanation: With several buses, multiple operand transfers happen in parallel in a single step (for example, two source registers can drive the ALU inputs while the result is written back), so an instruction finishes in fewer clock cycles. A single bus forces those transfers to be serialised, one per cycle. The bus count does not change register size, so B is a distractor.

stem: The ISA standard Buses are used to connect ___________
explanation: ISA expansion slots carried the disk controller cards (MFM, then IDE), which is how the hard disk reached the processor on PC/AT-class machines. RAM never used ISA (it sits on the dedicated memory bus), and GPU and optical drives belong to the later PCI/AGP and IDE era. Legacy item.

stem: The three main parts of central processing unit (CPU processor) is
explanation: The CPU is defined as ALU (arithmetic and logic) plus control unit (fetch, decode, sequencing) plus registers (fast internal working storage). RAM and cache are memory outside that definition, which rules out B, C and D.

stem: Which functional component of the computer system is responsible for the computing?
explanation: The CPU is the component that actually executes instructions and performs arithmetic and logic, so it is what does the computing. RAM only holds data and instructions, and input devices only supply them, so 'both a and b' is wrong.

stem: Which of the following is the brain of any computer system?
explanation: The CPU is conventionally called the brain of the computer because it fetches, decodes and executes every instruction. The trap is picking ALU or control unit: those are parts inside the CPU, not the whole.

stem: The hardware in which data is stored permanently for a computer system is
explanation: Secondary memory (hard disk, SSD, optical media) is non-volatile, so its contents survive power-off, which is what 'stored permanently' means here. Registers and main memory (RAM) are volatile and lose their contents when power is removed.

stem: Which of the following is the main circuit board of computer system unit?
explanation: The motherboard is the main circuit board of the system unit; the CPU, RAM and expansion cards all plug into it. Registers, CPU and RAM are components mounted on it, not the board itself.

stem: The central processing unit and memory are located on the
explanation: The CPU socket and the memory slots are both mounted directly on the motherboard. An expansion board is an add-in card that plugs into the motherboard, and a storage device is not a board at all.

stem: Motherboard is also known as
explanation: A motherboard is physically a printed circuit board (PCB), also called the mainboard or system board. The other three option names are not real terms.

stem: Conversion of computer-generated results into human-readable format is the function of
explanation: Results leave the CPU in machine form (binary), and the output unit or interface converts them into a human-readable form such as screen text or print. The input interface does the reverse conversion, and CPU and RAM only process and hold the data.

stem: What does the control unit generate to control other units?
explanation: Answer unverified: the source PDF prints no key, and the item has two defensible readings. Hamacher's textbook phrasing is that the control unit generates the timing signals that govern all transfers, which is why 'Timing signals' is often keyed. But timing fundamentally comes from the clock and answers when, not what does the controlling, and the stem's qualifier 'to control other units' points at 'Control signals': the control unit is the nerve centre that asserts control signals to the ALU, memory and I/O. Adjudication settled on Control signals. 'Transfer signals' and 'command signals' are not terms of art.

stem: Which of the following bus structure is used to connect I/O devices?
explanation: In the classic single-bus structure the processor, memory and all I/O devices hang off one common bus, so I/O connects there. Star bus is a network topology, not a CPU bus, and Rambus is a DRAM interface. The item is loosely worded, but single bus is the intended answer.

stem: In the CPU, what is the functionality of the control unit?

options.label: D
options.text: To transfer data to primary storage

explanation: The control unit fetches instructions and decodes them, then issues the control signals that sequence the rest of the CPU. Logic operations are the ALU's job (B) and storage is memory's (C), which is the usual mix-up.

stem: Which of the following is known as the step by step procedure to solve a problem?
explanation: An algorithm is by definition a finite, step-by-step procedure for solving a problem. Graphs and tables are data representations, not procedures.

stem: What does EEPROM stands for?

options.label: A
options.text: Electrically Erasable and Programmable Read-Only Memory

options.label: B
options.text: Electronically Erasable and Programmable Read-Only Memory

options.label: C
options.text: Electrically Enabled and Programmable Read-Only Memory

explanation: EEPROM = Electrically Erasable Programmable Read-Only Memory, so erased electrically rather than by UV light (that would be EPROM). The trap is 'Electronically', which sounds right but is not the standard expansion.

stem: CISC stands for -
explanation: CISC = Complex Instruction Set Computer, the design style with many multi-step instructions and variable-length encodings (x86). Its counterpart is RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer), with few fixed-length, single-cycle instructions.

stem: Which of the following building block can be used to implement any combinational logic circuit?
explanation: NAND is functionally complete (a universal gate): NOT a = NAND(a,a), AND = NAND followed by that inverter, and OR = NAND of the two inverted inputs by De Morgan. AND or OR alone cannot produce inversion, so no set of only-AND or only-OR gates can implement every combinational function. NOR is also universal but is not offered here.

stem: Which of the following is a combinational logic circuit that has 2 n input lines and a single output line?
explanation: A multiplexer has 2^n data inputs plus n select lines and routes exactly one of them to a single output. The trap is the inverse circuits: a demultiplexer and a decoder take one input to 2^n outputs, and an encoder takes 2^n inputs to n outputs, not to one line.

stem: The address in the main memory is known as :
explanation: The physical address is the real location in main memory that the memory unit actually sees on the address bus. A logical (virtual) address is what the CPU generates; the MMU translates it into a physical address at run time.

stem: Which of the following circuit is used to store one bit of data?
explanation: A flip-flop is the basic bistable element, holding exactly one bit. Register is the classic trap: a register is a group of n flip-flops and stores n bits, so it is not the one-bit storage circuit. Decoders and encoders are combinational and store nothing.

stem: What is the full form of DBMS?
explanation: DBMS = Database Management System.

stem: What is DBMS?

options.label: A
options.text: DBMS is a collection of queries

options.label: D
options.text: DBMS stores, modifies and retrieves data

explanation: A DBMS is the software layer that stores, modifies and retrieves data on behalf of users and applications. It is not a language: SQL is the language, and the DBMS is the system that executes it.

stem: Which of the following is not a type of database?
explanation: Hierarchical, network and distributed are all standard database classifications; "decentralized" is not part of the usual taxonomy, so it is the odd one out. Weak item: the distinction between decentralized and distributed is terminology, not reasoning, so learn the keyed list rather than trying to derive it.

stem: Which of the following is a component of the DBMS?
explanation: The standard component list for a DBMS covers data, the data languages (DDL, DML, DCL), the data manager (database engine), plus hardware, users and procedures. All three listed items are components, so "All of the above" is correct.

stem: The ability to query data, as well as insert, delete, and alter tuples, is offered by ____________
explanation: Querying rows plus INSERT, DELETE and UPDATE are all DML operations, because they act on the tuples inside a table. The distractors act elsewhere: DDL defines the schema (CREATE, ALTER, DROP), DCL handles privileges (GRANT, REVOKE), and TCL handles transactions (COMMIT, ROLLBACK).

stem: Why the following statement is erroneous?

options.label: A
options.text: Dept_id should not be used in group by clause

options.label: B
options.text: Group by clause is not valid in this query

explanation: Any property that does not occur in the group by clause must only appear in an aggregate function if it also appears in the select clause; otherwise, the query is considered incorrect.

stem: What is the full form of DML?
explanation: DML = Data Manipulation Language (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE).

stem: Which of the following is a feature of the database?
explanation: Keyed B: providing a user/query interface is a general feature of a database system. A and C are anti-features (no backup, lack of authentication), and D describes distributed databases specifically rather than databases in general, which is the trap here.

stem: What does an RDBMS consist of?
explanation: RDBMS is the relational model, which organizes all data as a collection of tables (relations). Records, fields and keys are the parts inside one table, not what the system as a whole is composed of.

stem: Which one of the following given statements possibly contains the error?

options.label: B
options.text: select empid from emp where empid = 10006;

options.label: D
options.text: select empid where empid = 1009 and Lastname = 'GELLER';

explanation: The Query given in option D does not contain the "from" clause, which specifies the relation from which the values have to be selected or fetched. Therefore the correct answer is D.

stem: The term "FAT" is stands for_____
explanation: FAT = File Allocation Table, the on-disk structure that records which clusters belong to which file and chains them together.

stem: Which one of the following is not true?

options.label: A
options.text: kernel remains in the memory during the entire computer session

options.label: B
options.text: kernel is made of various modules which can not be loaded in running operating system

options.label: C
options.text: kernel is the first part of the operating system to load into memory during booting

options.label: D
options.text: kernel is the program that constitutes the central core of the operating system

explanation: Kernel is the first program that is loaded in memory when OS is loading as well as it remains in memory till OS is running. Kernel is the core part of the OS which is responsible for managing resources, allowing multiple processes to use the resources and provide services to various processes. Kernel modules can be loaded and unloaded in run-time i.e. in running OS.

stem: What does OS X has?
explanation: OS X has a hybrid kernel. Hybrid kernel is a combination of two different kernels. OS X is developed by Apple and originally it is known as Mac OS X.

stem: In operating system, each process has its own __________

options.label: B
options.text: pending alarms, signals, and signal handlers

explanation: All three are per-process resources: the process owns its address space and global variables, its own open-file table, and its own pending alarms, signals and signal handlers, so "all of the mentioned" is correct. The contrast to remember is threads, which share all of these within a single process and keep only their own stack, registers and program counter.

stem: When a process is in a “Blocked” state waiting for some I/O service. When the service is completed, it goes to the __________
explanation: I/O completion only removes the reason the process was blocked; it does not give it the CPU. The process moves Blocked -> Ready and waits for the scheduler to dispatch it. "Running" is the classic trap: nothing enters Running except by the dispatcher picking it off the ready queue.

stem: The FCFS algorithm is particularly troublesome for ____________
explanation: FCFS is non-preemptive and has no time quantum, so one long CPU-bound job holds the processor until it finishes. That destroys response time in a time-sharing system, where every interactive user must get the CPU back at regular short intervals.

stem: The main memory accommodates ____________
explanation: Main memory is partitioned into a region for the resident operating system and a region for user processes, and the graded key is the operating system. "All of the mentioned" is wrong because the CPU is hardware and is never stored in memory. Note the item is loose: user processes do also occupy main memory, so treat the key as the textbook phrasing rather than a strict fact.

stem: The _________ presents a uniform device-access interface to the I/O subsystem, much as system calls provide a standard interface between the application and the operating system.
explanation: The device driver is the layer that hides the hardware differences of each device and exposes one uniform set of operations (read, write, seek) upward. That is the same role system calls play between applications and the kernel. Buses and devices are the hardware being abstracted, not the abstraction.

stem: What are the characteristics of stack based IDS?

options.label: A
options.text: It is programmed to interpret a certain series of packets

options.label: B
options.text: It models the normal usage of the network as a noise characterization

options.label: C
options.text: They are integrated closely with the TCP/IP stack and watch packets

options.label: D
options.text: The host operating system logs in the audit information

explanation: A stack-based IDS is hooked into the TCP/IP stack itself, so it inspects packets as they move up the layers and can drop them before the OS or application ever sees them. The distractors describe other IDS types: interpreting a series of packets is signature-based, modeling normal traffic as noise is anomaly-based, and reading OS audit logs is host-based.

stem: The operating system keeps a small table containing information about all open files called ____________
explanation: The OS keeps an open-file table holding an entry per currently open file (location on disk, access rights, open count), so it does not have to re-search the directory on every read or write. The directory structure on disk is separate; the in-memory table of open files is the open-file table.

stem: Whenever a process needs I/O to or from a disk it issues a ______________

options.label: A
options.text: system call to the operating system

explanation: Disk access is a privileged operation, so a user process cannot touch the controller directly. It issues a system call, which traps into kernel mode and lets the OS perform the transfer. "System call to the CPU" is nonsense: system calls are requests to the operating system, not to hardware.

stem: In Unix, which system call creates the new process?
explanation: fork() is the Unix process-creation call: it duplicates the calling process, returning 0 in the child and the child's PID in the parent. exec() then replaces the child's image with a new program; there is no create() or new() system call in Unix.

stem: What is the full name of FAT?
explanation: FAT stands for File Allocation Table, the on-disk table that chains the clusters belonging to each file.

stem: Banker's algorithm is used?
explanation: Banker's algorithm is used to prevent the deadlock condition. The banker algorithm is sometimes called the detection algorithm. It is named the banker algorithm because it is used to determine whether a loan can be granted in the banking system or not.

stem: What is the full name of the DSM?
explanation: DSM stands for Distributed Shared Memory: an abstraction that lets processes on separate machines address what looks like one shared memory space, with the middleware handling the underlying message passing.

stem: What is the full name of the IDL?
explanation: IDL stands for Interface Definition Language, the language-neutral notation (used by CORBA, RPC, COM) that declares an object's interface so client stubs and server skeletons can be generated for different programming languages.

stem: The size of virtual memory is based on which of the following?
explanation: Virtual memory size is capped by how many distinct addresses the CPU can form, which is the width of the address bus: n address lines give 2^n addressable bytes, so a 32-bit address bus gives 4 GB. The trap is RAM: installed RAM limits physical memory, not the virtual address space, which is the whole point of virtual memory.

stem: Which of the following is not application software?
explanation: Windows 7 is an operating system, which is system software: it manages the hardware and hosts everything else. WordPad, Photoshop and MS Excel are all application software running on top of it.

stem: Which of the following operating systems does not support more than one program at a time?
explanation: DOS stands for Disk operating system. Disk operating system is a single-user operating system that does not support more than one program at a time.

stem: FIFO scheduling is
explanation: FIFO (also called FCFS) dispatches processes in arrival order and lets each one run until it finishes or blocks, so the CPU is never taken away by the scheduler. That is the definition of non-preemptive scheduling.

stem: The output of an AND gate with three inputs, A, B, and C, is HIGH when ________.
explanation: An AND gate outputs 1 only when every input is 1, so A = B = C = 1 is the single HIGH row of the truth table. Any input at 0 forces the output to 0.

stem: Output will be a LOW for any case when one or more inputs are zero for a(n):
explanation: The AND gate is the one whose output goes LOW as soon as any single input is 0, since 0 is the dominant value for AND. The NOR trap: NOR outputs HIGH (not LOW) when all inputs are 0, so it fails the condition.

stem: TTL operates from a ________.
explanation: Standard TTL logic runs on a nominal +5 V Vcc (typically 4.75 to 5.25 V). The 3 V figure belongs to low-voltage CMOS families, not TTL.

stem: The Boolean expression for a 3-input AND gate is ________.
explanation: AND is the Boolean product, so a 3-input AND gate is X = ABC (X is 1 only when A, B and C are all 1). X = A + B + C is the OR expression, the classic sum-versus-product mix-up.

stem: The output of logic circuit given below represents ______ gate.
explanation: The circuit is NAND built out of NOR gates: the first two NORs are wired as inverters, NOR(A,A) = A' and NOR(B,B) = B'; feeding those into a NOR gives (A' + B')' = A.B by De Morgan; the final NOR inverts that to (A.B)', which is NAND. The trap is stopping one gate early at A.B and answering AND.

stem: If A = 1, B = 1, what will be the values of Y and Z?
explanation: Trace both outputs: Y = A'.(A+B) and Z = Y + (A+B). With A = 1, B = 1: A' = 0, so Y = 0.(1) = 0; A + B = 1, so Z = 0 + 1 = 1. Hence Y = 0, Z = 1.

stem: Which of the following methods provides dedicated communication channel between two stations?
explanation: Circuit switching reserves an end-to-end path (the classic telephone call) that stays dedicated to the two stations for the whole session. Packet switching is the distractor: it shares every link statistically and gives no dedicated channel.

stem: The most flexibility how devices are wired together is provided by
explanation: In a bus topology any device can tap the shared trunk cable at any point, so there is no central hub to home-run to and no closed loop to maintain, giving the most freedom in how the wiring is laid out. The question is loosely worded, but star and ring both impose a fixed cabling structure.

stem: Which of the following items is not used in local area network (LAN)?
explanation: A modem modulates data onto a telephone/WAN line to reach outside the site, so it belongs to the WAN side, not the LAN itself. Cable, printer and computer are all ordinary LAN components.

stem: A proxy server is used as the computer?
explanation: A proxy server is the intermediary machine that holds the external (Internet) connection and makes outbound requests on behalf of internal clients, so the internal hosts never talk to the outside directly. Backup, file handling and permissions are jobs of other servers.

stem: Which software prevents the external access to a system?
explanation: A firewall inspects traffic against a rule set and blocks unauthorised inbound connections, which is exactly 'preventing external access'. Routers and gateways forward traffic rather than filter it, and a virus checker targets malware already on the machine.

stem: Which one of the following would breach the integrity of a system?

options.label: A
options.text: Looking the room to prevent theft

options.label: B
options.text: Full access rights for all users

options.label: C
options.text: Fitting the system with an anti-theft device

options.label: D
options.text: Protecting the device against willful or accidental damage

explanation: Integrity means data cannot be altered by unauthorised parties. Giving every user full access rights lets anyone modify or delete data, so integrity is breached. The other options are physical security measures, which protect availability and the hardware, not data correctness.

stem: The maximum length (in bytes) of an IPv4 datagram is?
explanation: The IPv4 header's Total Length field is 16 bits, so the largest value it can hold is 2^16 - 1 = 65535, and that count covers header plus payload in bytes. 512 and 1024 are typical MTU-ish numbers, not the protocol maximum.

stem: What IP address class allocates 8 bits for the host identification part?
explanation: Class C splits the 32-bit address as 24 network bits + 8 host bits, giving 2^8 - 2 = 254 usable hosts per network. Class B leaves 16 host bits and Class A leaves 24; Class D is multicast and has no host part at all.

stem: The term IANA stands for?
explanation: IANA stands for Internet Assigned Numbers Authority, the body that allocates IP address blocks, protocol numbers and port numbers.

stem: How many versions available of IP?
explanation: Only two IP versions are actually deployed: IPv4 and IPv6. The trap is reading the '6' in IPv6 as a count of versions; it is a version number, not a total.

stem: Which layer of the TCP / IP stack corresponds to the OSI model transport layer?
explanation: The TCP/IP stack's host-to-host layer (where TCP and UDP live) provides end-to-end delivery between processes, which is precisely OSI layer 4, the transport layer. Internet maps to OSI network (layer 3) and Network Access maps to OSI data link plus physical.

stem: Which of the following algorithms is not used in asymmetric-key cryptography?
explanation: ECB (Electronic Code Book) is a mode of operation for symmetric block ciphers such as DES/AES, where both parties share one secret key. RSA and Diffie-Hellman are the genuine public-key (asymmetric) algorithms, so ECB is the odd one out.

stem: A network router joins two _________ together?
explanation: A router works at layer 3 and forwards packets between two or more distinct IP networks, using the destination network address to pick the next hop. Joining computers or switches within one network is a switch/hub's job at layer 2.

stem: Message Oriented Middleware allows general purpose messages to be exchanged in a Client/Server system using message queues.
explanation: True. That is the standard definition of Message Oriented Middleware: clients and servers exchange general-purpose messages through queues, which decouples them and allows asynchronous communication (the sender does not block waiting for the receiver).

stem: Which of the following below is a loopback IP address?
explanation: 127.0.0.1 is the canonical loopback host address, the one a machine uses to talk to itself. 127.0.0.0 is the network address of the reserved 127.0.0.0/8 loopback block, not a host address, which is the trap here.

stem: Ping command works on which protocol?
explanation: Ping sends an ICMP Echo Request and waits for an ICMP Echo Reply, timing the round trip. ICMP rides directly on IP; there is no TCP or UDP transport involved.

stem: Which of the following protocols below work in the application layer?
explanation: POP (mail retrieval) and FTP (file transfer) are both application-layer protocols, while PPP is a data-link (layer 2) protocol, so the combined option is correct. Note the option text is misprinted: 'Both a and d' should read 'both a and c', meaning POP and FTP.

stem: What is the port number for HTTP?
explanation: HTTP is well-known port 80 (HTTPS is 443). The distractors are the other classic well-known ports you are expected to have memorised: 25 is SMTP and 21 is FTP control; 65 is not a standard service port at all.

stem: MAC addresses are also known as?
explanation: A MAC address is the 48-bit identifier burned into the NIC, so it is called both the hardware address and the physical address; since options A and B are each individually true, the only complete answer is C. The trap is D: an IP address is the logical Layer 3 address, assigned by software and changeable, which is exactly what a MAC address is not.

stem: A network point that provides entrance into another network is called as ___________
explanation: A gateway is by definition the node that acts as the entrance from one network into another, translating between them where the protocols differ. Router is the near-miss: a router forwards packets between networks that already speak the same protocol, and a switch only moves frames inside one LAN.

stem: UDP is an unreliable protocol.
explanation: True. UDP is connectionless: it sends datagrams with no handshake, no acknowledgements, no retransmission and no ordering guarantee, so delivery is best-effort. That unreliability is the point: it buys low latency and low overhead, which is why voice, video and DNS use it.

stem: A stream socket provides _________ communications between a client and a server
explanation: A stream socket is the TCP socket type (SOCK_STREAM), and TCP connections are full-duplex, so data flows in both directions at once between client and server. One-way would describe a simplex link, not a TCP stream.

stem: Token Ring is a data link technology for?
explanation: Token Ring (IEEE 802.5) is a LAN data-link technology: stations pass a token around a ring inside one site, the same scope as Ethernet. It is a LAN access method, not a carrier technology for WAN or MAN distances.

stem: TELNET used _________ protocol for data connection
explanation: TELNET opens a TCP connection (port 23) because a remote terminal session needs reliable, in-order delivery of every keystroke. UDP is the trap: losing or reordering characters in a login session would be unusable. IP is a Layer 3 protocol, not a transport, and DHCP is an address-assignment service.

stem: What is the full form of URL?
explanation: URL stands for Uniform Resource Locator. The distractors swap 'Uniform' for 'Universal' or 'Resource' for 'router/routing'; the correct term is Uniform Resource Locator, matching URI (Uniform Resource Identifier).

stem: The output Qn of of a J-K flip-flop is 0. It changes to 1 when a clock pulse is applied. The inputs Jn and Kn are respectively.
explanation: Use the JK excitation table for the required transition Q 0 -> 1: J must be 1, K is don't-care (X), because with J=1 the flip-flop either sets (K=0) or toggles from 0 (K=1), and both land on 1. So Jn, Kn = 1, X. Options with J=0 cannot drive the output high, and 'X and 1' would be the answer for the 1 -> 0 transition instead.

stem: The output Qn of a J-K (or S-R) flip-flop is 0. Its output does not change when a clock pulse is applied. The inputs Jn and Kn (or Sn-Rn) are respectively
explanation: Required transition is Q 0 -> 0, so from the JK excitation table J=0 and K is don't-care (X): with J=0 the flip-flop either holds (K=0) or resets (K=1), and both leave the output at 0. So Jn, Kn = 0, X. Answer C (1 and X) is the trap, since that is the entry for the 0 -> 1 transition.

stem: Which of the following retains the information it's storing when the power to the system is turned off?
explanation: ROM is non-volatile: its contents survive a power cut, which is why the boot firmware lives there. RAM (and a DIMM, which is just a RAM module) is volatile and loses everything at power-off, and the CPU is a processor, not storage.

stem: A network of computers and other devices that is confined to a relatively small space is called?
explanation: 'Confined to a relatively small space' (one building, office or campus) is the definition of a Local Area Network. A WAN spans cities or countries, and peer-to-peer describes how hosts relate to each other, not the geographic size of the network.

stem: Every computer connected to the Internet is identified by a unique four-part string, known as
explanation: The 'unique four-part string' is the dotted-quad IPv4 address, e.g. 192.168.1.1, four octets separated by dots. A domain name or host name is the human-readable label that DNS resolves into that address, and neither has a fixed four-part form.

stem: ENIAC was the first general-purpose electronic computer. ENIAC stands for
explanation: ENIAC stands for Electronic Numerical Integrator and Computer (1946, University of Pennsylvania). The distractors substitute 'Network' or 'Automatic'; the acronym is Numerical Integrator and Computer.

stem: Which of the following statement is correct?
explanation: Storage units here are binary powers of two: 1 KB = 2^10 = 1024 bytes, so A is correct. The rest fail that rule: 1 MB = 1024 KB = 1,048,576 bytes, not 2048 bytes and not 1000 KB, and D uses the decimal 1000-byte definition that this exam does not want.

stem: .PNG refers to
explanation: PNG stands for Portable Network Graphics, a lossless raster image format, so a .PNG file is an image file.

stem: USB is a device used to store data and it stands for
explanation: USB stands for Universal Serial Bus. It is 'serial' because it transfers one bit at a time over a differential pair, and 'universal' because one bus standard serves keyboards, drives, cameras and so on.

stem: _____ is a technology that allows telephone calls to be made over computer networks like the Internet.
explanation: VoIP (Voice over IP) digitises speech into packets and carries the call over an IP network such as the Internet instead of the circuit-switched phone network. GSM and CDMA are cellular radio standards and a modem is only the hardware that puts data on a line, so none of them is the technology being described.

stem: A program that runs in the background on your computer, sending information about your browsing habits to the company that installed it on your computer is called as
explanation: Answer unverified: the printed key marks C (Grayware), but the stem describes software that hides in the background and reports your browsing habits back to whoever installed it, which is the textbook definition of D, Spyware. Grayware is only an umbrella term for unwanted-but-not-strictly-malicious software (it covers spyware and adware both), so it is the vaguer option rather than the specific category named; adware is defined by displaying advertisements, which the stem never mentions. Learn the real distinction, but note that this exam's key says Grayware.

stem: Software is a set of programs, which is designed to perform a well defined function and there are two types of software, application and system. The following are all application software except for
explanation: The question asks which is NOT application software. Microsoft Windows is the operating system, i.e. system software: it manages the hardware and provides the platform that Word, Excel and PowerPoint (all applications performing user-facing tasks) run on top of.

stem: 1 Gigabyte (GB) is equal to
explanation: The binary ladder steps by 1024 each rung: 1 KB = 1024 bytes, 1 MB = 1024 KB, 1 GB = 1024 MB. Option A (1024 bytes) is the definition of a kilobyte, not a gigabyte, and 1024 TB would be a petabyte, so the only rung that lands on GB is 1024 MB.

stem: The fourth generation computers are based on
explanation: Generations track the switching technology: 1st = vacuum tubes, 2nd = transistors, 3rd = integrated circuits, 4th = VLSI microprocessors (Intel 4004 onward, whole CPU on one chip), 5th = ULSI and AI machines. Transistor and IC are the previous two generations, and ULSI (option D) is the classic trap because it is the next one, not the fourth.

stem: All the following are computer input devices except for
explanation: A printer takes data from the computer and renders it on paper, so it is an output device. Keyboard, mouse and joystick all feed data into the computer, so they are inputs.

stem: A CD-RW has a speed rating of 12x 10x 32x. What do the three numbers refer to in order?
explanation: CD-RW drives are rated in write x rewrite x read order, so 12x 10x 32x means 12x write, 10x rewrite, 32x read. The shortcut for the exam: the read speed is always the largest of the three numbers because reading is the fastest operation, so the 32x has to sit in the last position, which rules out every other ordering.

stem: The information stored in _______ is erased when the computer is turned off
explanation: RAM is volatile: its cells need power to hold their charge, so contents are lost when the machine is switched off. ROM and the BIOS chip are non-volatile and keep their contents without power, and the CPU is a processor, not storage.

stem: Malware is the short form for malicious software and used to refer to
explanation: Malware is an umbrella term for any malicious software, so spyware, worms and viruses are all instances of it rather than alternatives to it. That makes 'all of the above' the only answer that covers the definition.

stem: What is the best way to protect your hard drive data?
explanation: A backup is a second copy on separate media, so it is the only listed action that survives drive failure, ransomware or accidental deletion. Scanning detects malware, defragmentation reorganises files for speed and deleting junk frees space, but none of them preserve the data if the drive dies.

stem: The following device is used to connect two systems using different protocols
explanation: A gateway is the protocol translator: it can reach up to the application layer and convert between dissimilar protocols or architectures. A repeater only regenerates the signal (layer 1), a bridge forwards frames between segments running the same protocol (layer 2), and a modem converts digital to analogue, none of which translate protocols.

stem: The computer language used for artificial intelligence is
explanation: Answer unverified: the exam PDF keys option C (the C language), but that is almost certainly a keying error, and the same file miskeys several consecutive items to C. The defensible answer is B, PROLOG (PROgramming in LOGic), the classic AI language alongside LISP. FORTRAN is for numeric computation, COBOL for business data processing, and C is a general-purpose systems language with no AI-specific constructs.

stem: Open source software is software whose source code is available for modification or enhancement by anyone. Which of the following is an example of open source software?
explanation: LibreOffice is released under a free/open licence with its source code publicly available and modifiable. Photoshop, Microsoft Word and Skype are all proprietary, closed-source products.

stem: JPEG seen with the .jpg or .jpeg filename extension is commonly used method for compression of digital images. What does JPEG stand for?
explanation: JPEG stands for Joint Photographic Experts Group, the committee that defined the standard. The other options are invented expansions built from plausible-sounding words.

stem: A “URL” is a formatted text string used by web browsers, email clients and other software to identify a network resource on the Internet. It stands for
explanation: URL stands for Uniform Resource Locator: 'uniform' because the syntax is the same for every scheme, and 'locator' because it says where the resource is and how to fetch it. The distractors swap in 'Universal' or 'Link', which belong to no standard.

stem: SD Card is a memory cards used in electronic equipments including digital cameras and cell phones.  What does SD stands for?
explanation: SD stands for Secure Digital, the flash memory card standard from the SD Association. 'Scan Disk' is the trap: it echoes the old ScanDisk disk-checking utility, which has nothing to do with memory cards.

stem: A byte is equivalent to
explanation: A byte is defined as 8 bits, which gives 2^8 = 256 distinct values, enough for the extended ASCII character set.

stem: A standard computer keyboard has how many keys?
explanation: The standard Windows enhanced keyboard has 104 keys: the older 101-key layout plus two Windows keys and one menu key. The 84-key figure is the obsolete IBM AT layout.

stem: HTTP stands for:
explanation: HTTP stands for HyperText Transfer Protocol, the application-layer protocol browsers use to request and receive web pages. The other options scramble the words into expansions that do not exist.

stem: The capacity of 3-1/2 inch floppy is:
explanation: The formatted capacity of a 3.5 inch high-density floppy disk is 1.44 MB. Legacy trivia: the other options are near-miss numbers with no basis.

stem: A character has how many bits:
explanation: In ASCII and extended ASCII, one character is stored in one byte, which is 8 bits. Note this is the exam's convention: Unicode encodings such as UTF-8 can use up to 4 bytes per character, but this question is asking for the classic one-byte-per-character rule.

stem: A program that allows us to do specific tasks is:
explanation: Application software is written to perform specific user tasks (word processing, spreadsheets, browsing). System software, which includes operating systems such as Windows 2000 and Windows XP, manages the hardware and provides the platform that applications run on, so options B, C and D are all the same wrong category.

stem: In a computer network which device acts as a traffic cop:
explanation: Answer unverified: the exam PDF keys Switch, but the standard 'traffic cop' metaphor (deciding which way traffic goes at a junction) describes the Router, which selects paths and forwards packets between different networks. A switch only forwards frames by MAC address inside a single LAN, a hub blindly floods every port, and a modem just modulates the signal. Published versions of this stem answer Router, and this source file miskeys several items to C, so treat Router as the intended answer but know both readings.

stem: The computer you are controlling or working via a network is called?
explanation: The machine you operate across the network is the remote computer; the local computer is the one physically in front of you that you are typing on. 'Host' is the trap: every machine attached to a network is a host, so it does not distinguish the two ends of the session.

stem: WYSIWYG is an acronym for what?

options.label: A
options.text: What You Saw Is What You Got

options.label: B
options.text: What You See Is What You Get

options.label: C
options.text: What You Seen Is What You Gotten

options.label: D
options.text: What You Sow Is What You Get

explanation: WYSIWYG stands for What You See Is What You Get: the editing view on screen matches the printed or published output. The other options are made-up tense variations of the same phrase.

stem: Which protocol sends electronic mail?
explanation: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) is the push protocol that sends mail from a client to a mail server and relays it between servers. POP3 is the trap: it only retrieves mail from a mailbox. FTP transfers files, and Outlook Express is a client application, not a protocol.

stem: What type of connector is used to plug a telephone line into a modem?
explanation: RJ-11 is the small 6-position jack used for analogue telephone lines, which is what plugs into a dial-up modem. RJ-45 is the classic trap: it is the 8-position Ethernet connector, physically wider and used for LAN cabling.

stem: From what location are the 1st computer instructions available on boot up?
explanation: At power-on RAM is empty, so the CPU fetches its very first instruction from the reset vector, which is mapped to firmware held in ROM BIOS. boot.ini and CONFIG.SYS are files on disk, and reading a disk already requires running code, so they cannot be first.

stem: What could cause a fixed disk error.
explanation: 'Fixed disk' means the hard disk. Legacy BIOS stores the drive type and geometry in CMOS, so if those settings are wrong or the CMOS battery has failed, POST cannot address the drive and reports a fixed disk error. Bad RAM or a slow processor produce entirely different symptoms.

stem: Missing slot covers on a computer can cause?
explanation: Slot covers seal the case so the fans pull air along the intended path across the boards; with covers missing the airflow short-circuits and the machine overheats (and dust gets in). EMI is a defensible real-world second answer because the covers are part of the shield, but overheating is the keyed one.

stem: When installing PCI NICS you can check the IRQ availability by looking at
explanation: PCI cards are jumperless and configured by Plug and Play, so there are no dip switches or jumpers to read. IRQ assignment and availability are shown in the motherboard BIOS setup under PnP/PCI configuration. CONFIG.SYS is a DOS text file and tracks no interrupts.

stem: With respect to a network interface card, the term 10/100 refers to
explanation: 10/100 means the NIC negotiates either 10 Mbps or 100 Mbps Ethernet, so the numbers are megabits per second. It is a link speed on copper (10BASE-T / 100BASE-TX), not a fiber rate and not a property of the protocol.

stem: Which Motherboard form factor uses one 20 pin connector
explanation: ATX introduced the single keyed 20-pin motherboard power connector. AT and Baby AT used two separate 6-pin connectors (P8 and P9) that could be swapped and plugged in backwards, which is why the ATX design replaced them.

stem: A hard disk is divided into tracks which are further subdivided into:
explanation: Each platter surface has concentric tracks, and every track is divided into sectors, the smallest physically addressable unit on the disk (classically 512 bytes). Clusters are the trap: they are groups of sectors defined by the file system, not a subdivision of the disk geometry.

stem: A wrist grounding strap contains which of the following:
explanation: An ESD wrist strap has roughly a 1 megohm resistor in series with its ground lead. That bleeds static charge off you slowly instead of instantly, and limits current to a safe level if you touch a live conductor while grounded.

stem: Which standard govern parallel communications?
explanation: IEEE 1284 is the standard for the PC parallel (printer) port, covering SPP, EPP and ECP modes. RS-232 is the trap: it is the serial standard, and CAT 5 is a cable specification rather than a communications standard.

stem: In laser printer technology, what happens during the conditioning stage?

options.label: A
options.text: The corona wire places a uniform positive charge on the paper

options.label: B
options.text: A uniform negative charge is placed on the photosensitive drum

options.label: C
options.text: A uniform negative charge is placed on the toner

explanation: Conditioning (charging) is the stage where the primary corona wire or charge roller applies a uniform negative charge across the whole photosensitive drum; the laser then selectively discharges the areas that will pick up toner. Charging the paper is the transfer stage, not conditioning.

stem: What product is used to clean smudged keys on a keyboard?
explanation: Smudges on keycaps are ordinary grime, so a mild all-purpose cleaner on a cloth is what the bank keys as correct. Solvents and silicone spray can attack the plastic and the printed legends, and denatured alcohol is intended for contacts rather than casework.

stem: ESD would cause the most damage to which component?
explanation: ESD destroys low-voltage semiconductor logic, which sits exposed on expansion boards; a static discharge too small for you to feel is already enough to damage a chip. Power supplies, monitors and keyboards handle far higher voltages and currents and are comparatively rugged.

stem: To view any currently running Terminate Stay Resident (TSR's) programs you could type:
explanation: The DOS MEM command (usually MEM /C) lists the programs currently loaded in conventional and upper memory, which is exactly how you see resident TSRs. MEMMAKER is the trap: it optimizes memory usage rather than reporting it, and 'Memory' is not a DOS command at all.

stem: Which type of system board is the MOST likely candidate for processor upgrading if you want maximum performance and future compatibility?
explanation: Of the buses listed, PCI is the fast, modern one, so a PCI-based system board is the candidate that will not bottleneck a faster processor and has the best chance of taking future cards. ISA and EISA are legacy low-bandwidth buses.

stem: Suppose that you have a the maintenance package identifies several possible field replaceable units (FRUs) that will resolve the problem. What should you do after turning the power off?

options.label: A
options.text: Replace the indicated parts, one at a time in the recommended sequence, until the problem is resolved; return unused FRUs to stock

options.label: B
options.text: Replace all of the indicated FRUs at once and return the machine to the customer if the problem is resolved

options.label: C
options.text: Follow the same procedure as in ays replace the system board first if it is on the list of possible FRUs If multiple FRUs are indicated, then software is the most likely source of the problem

explanation: Correct service practice is to swap the FRUs one at a time, in the sequence the maintenance package recommends, retesting after each swap so you learn which part actually failed, and to return the unused parts to stock. Replacing all of them at once may fix the machine but wastes good parts and never isolates the fault.

stem: UNIVAC is
explanation: There are no computers with the name as in other options. UNIVAC was the first general purpose electronic digital computer designed for commercial use, produced by Universal Accounting Company of John Mauchly and J.P.Eckert in 1951.

stem: CD-ROM stands for
explanation: There are no objects with the name as in other options. CD-ROM is a non-volatile optical data storage medium using the same physical format as audio compact disk, readable by a computer with a CD-ROM drive. The standard 12 cm diameter CD-ROM store about 660 megabytes.

stem: ALU is
explanation: ALU is a unit in Central Processing Unit in a computer system that is responsible for arithmetic calculations and logical operations. Apart from ALU, the CPU contains MU (Memory Unit) and CU (Control Unit).

stem: VGA is
explanation: VGA is a type of Graphics Adapter. Graphic Adapter is an electronic board that controls the display of a monitor. This device helps the motherboard to work with the monitor and in VGA and SVGA the last letter ‘A’ stands for ‘Array’ whereas in MDA, CGA, MCGA the last letter ‘A’ stands for ‘Adapter’.

stem: IBM 1401 is
explanation: IBM 1401 is a Second Generation Computer and is the first computer to enter Nepal in 2028 BS for census. Government of Nepal had brought this computer on rent and later purchased for data processing in Bureau of Statistics. After this computer, another ICL 2950/10, a British computer, was purchased by the fund of UNDP and UNFPA for the census of 2038 BS is second computer in Nepal.

stem: MSI stands for
explanation: After the invention of IC chips the development of computers plunged into next phase. Small Scale Integration and Medium Scale Integration (SSI and MSI) were used in third generation of computers and Large Scale Integration and Very Large Scale Integration (LSI and VLSI) are being used in fourth generation of computers. People are now expecting ULSI (Ultra Large Scale Integration) Circuits to be used for fifth generation computers.

stem: The capacity of 3.5 inch floppy disk is
explanation: Microfloppy disks (3.5 inch) if it is high density (MF2HD) can store 1.44 MB and if it is low density (MF2DD), it can store 720 KB. Mini Floppy disks (5.25 inch) if it is high density (MD2HD) can store 1.2 MB and low density (MD2DD) stores 360 KB of data.

stem: The first computer introduced in Nepal was
explanation: IBM 1401, a second generation computer was brought in Nepal by the Government of Nepal paying One Lakh and twenty five thousands per month to use in the census in 2028 B.S. Before this computer, Nepal was using a calculating device called Facit for statistical tasks.

stem: MICR stands for
explanation: MICR (Magnetic Ink Character Reader) is kind of scanner that can scan and identify the writing of magnetic ink. This device is used in banks to verify signatures in Checks.

stem: EBCDIC stands for

options.label: A
options.text: Extended Binary Coded Decimal Interchange Code

options.label: B
options.text: Extended Bit Code Decimal Interchange Code

options.label: C
options.text: Extended Bit Case Decimal Interchange Code

options.label: D
options.text: Extended Binary Case Decimal Interchange Code

explanation: EBCDIC is an 8-bit binary code for larger IBMs primarily mainframes in which each byte represent one alphanumeric character or two decimal digits. 256 characters can be coded using EBCDIC.

stem: BCD is
explanation: BCD is a binary coded notation in which each of the decimal digits is expressed as a 8-bit binary numeral. For example in binary coded decimal notation 12 is 0001 0010 as opposed to 1100 in pure binary.

stem: ASCII stands for

options.label: A
options.text: American Stable Code for International Interchange

options.label: B
options.text: American Standard Case for Institutional Interchange

options.label: C
options.text: American Standard Code for Information Interchange

options.label: D
options.text: American Standard Code for Interchange Information

explanation: ASCII is a code which converts characters – letters, digits, punctuations and control characters such as Alt, Tab etc – into numeral form. ASCII code is used to represent data internally in micro-computers. ASCII codes are 7 bits and can represent 0 to 127 and extended ASCII are 8 bits that represents 0 to 255.

stem: Which of the following is first generation of computer
explanation: IBM-1401, CDC-1604 is second generation computer. ICL-2900 is a fourth generation computer. EDSAC is important in the development of computer since it was the first computer to use John von. Neumann’s Stored Program Concept. It used 3000 vacuum tubes and computers with vacuum tubes are of first generation computers.

stem: Chief component of first generation computer was
explanation: Transistors were used for second generation computers and integrated circuits in third generation. First generation computers used vacuum tubes and valves as their main electronic component. Vacuum Tubes were invented by Lee DeForest in 1908.

stem: FORTRAN is
explanation: FORTRAN (Formula Translation) is one of the earlier High Level programming languages used to write scientific applications. It was developed by IBM in 1956.

stem: EEPROM stand for

options.label: A
options.text: Electrically Erasable Programmable Read Only Memory

options.label: B
options.text: Easily Erasable Programmable Read Only Memory

options.label: C
options.text: Electronic Erasable Programmable Read Only Memory

explanation: There are three types of ROM namely, PROM, EPROM and EEPROM. PROM can’t be reprogrammed, EPROM can be erased by exposing it in high intensity ultraviolet light and EEPROM can be erased and reprogrammed electrically. It is not needed to be removed from the computer to be modified.

stem: Second Generation computers were developed during
explanation: Second generation computers used transistors as their main electronic component. Transistor was invented by Bell Lab Scientists John Burdeen, Walter Brattain and William Shockley in 1947 and won the Nobel Prize in 1956 but it was not used in computers till 1956. The second generation continued until the implementation of IC chips invented by Jack Kilby in Texas Instruments in 1958.

stem: The computer size was very large in
explanation: It is obvious that computers developed with more power, reliability, speed and smaller sizes due to the enhancement of technology. First generation computers used 1000s of vacuum tubes that required lot of space made them gigantic in size. Single transistor could replace 1000 vacuum tubes and a single IC chip replaced 1000s of transistors made computers smaller and more speedy.

stem: Microprocessors as switching devices are for which generation computers
explanation: Microprocessors further revolutionized the development of computers. Personal microcomputers were possible due to the microprocessors. The first microprocessor called Intel 4004 was developed by American Intel Corporation in 1971. Microprocessors are used in the computers of fourth generation computers.

stem: Which of the following devices can be sued to directly image printed text?
explanation: OCR (Optical Character Reader) scans printed or typed text and converts the shapes of the characters into machine-readable text, so it images printed text directly. OMR only detects the presence of pencil marks in fixed positions (bubble sheets), and MICR only reads characters printed in magnetic ink (cheque routing numbers), so neither handles general printed text and 'All of above' is wrong.

stem: The output quality of a printer is measured by
explanation: Answer unverified: the circulated answer key marks B ('dot per sq. inch'), but print resolution is actually specified in DPI = dots per linear inch (300, 600, 1200 dpi), which is option A, and the review panel resolved to A. Option C is the classic trap: dots printed per unit time is printing speed (pages per minute), not output quality, which also rules out 'All of above'. If the grader uses the printed key, expect B; if it uses the real definition, expect A.

stem: In analog computer

options.label: A
options.text: Input is first converted to digital form

options.label: B
options.text: Input is never converted to digital form

options.label: C
options.text: Output is displayed in digital form

explanation: An analog computer represents values as continuous physical quantities (voltage, shaft rotation, fluid pressure) and computes on them directly, so the input is never converted to digital form and the output stays analog too. Options A and C describe a digital or hybrid machine, which makes 'All of above' wrong as well.

stem: In latest generation computers, the instructions are executed
explanation: Modern CPUs still fetch instructions in program order but overlap them with pipelining, superscalar issue, and multiple cores, so execution is both sequential and parallel. Picking 'Parallel only' is the trap: the programmer's model and the retirement order remain sequential even when the hardware runs several instructions at once.

stem: Who designed the first electronics computer – ENIAC?

options.label: C
options.text: J. Presper Eckert and John W Mauchly

explanation: ENIAC was designed and built by J. Presper Eckert and John W. Mauchly at the University of Pennsylvania. Von Neumann is the classic distractor: he joined the project later and is associated with the stored-program architecture of EDVAC, not with the design of ENIAC itself.

stem: Who invented the high level language c?
explanation: C was created by Dennis M. Ritchie at Bell Labs in the early 1970s. The distractors are each famous for something else: Niklaus Wirth for Pascal, Seymour Papert for Logo, Donald Knuth for TeX and The Art of Computer Programming.

stem: Personnel who design, program, operate and maintain computer equipment refers to
explanation: Peopleware is the standard term for the human component of a computing system, sitting alongside hardware and software. Programmer, console operator, and system analyst are each only one specific role within peopleware, so they are too narrow for a definition that spans design, programming, operation, and maintenance.

stem: When did arch rivals IBM and Apple Computers Inc. decide to join hands?
explanation: IBM and Apple joined forces in 1991 in what became the AIM alliance (Apple, IBM, Motorola), which produced the PowerPC chip and the Taligent and Kaleida ventures. 1984 is the trap: that is the Macintosh launch year, not the alliance.

stem: Human beings are referred to as Homosapinens, which device is called Sillico Sapiens?
explanation: 'Silico Sapiens' is a coined nickname for the computer, playing on Homo sapiens by swapping carbon-based thinking for silicon-based. Pure terminology trivia with no reasoning behind it.

stem: An error in software or hardware is called a bug. What is the alternative computer jargon for it?
explanation: Glitch is the alternative jargon for a bug, that is a fault in hardware or software. Leech, squid, and slug are not computing fault terms and exist here only as filler distractors.

stem: Modern Computer are very reliable but they are not
explanation: The stock phrasing is 'computers are reliable but not infallible': they execute correctly, but they still produce wrong results from wrong input or wrong programs (garbage in, garbage out). Fast, powerful, and cheap all describe what modern computers are, so only 'infallible' completes a negation.

stem: What is the name of the display feature that highlights are of the screen which requires operator attention?
explanation: Reverse video swaps the foreground and background colours of a screen region so it stands out and draws the operator's attention. A pixel is just the smallest addressable dot, a cursor only marks the input position, and a touch screen is an input technology, so none of them is a highlighting feature.

stem: IMB launched its first personal computer called IBM-PC in 1981. It had chips from Intel, disk drives from Tandon, operating system from Microsoft, the printer from Epson and the application software from everywhere. Can you name the country which contributed the video display?
explanation: The video display for the original 1981 IBM-PC was supplied from Taiwan. Legacy trivia with no reasoning behind it; memorize the pairing (Intel chips, Tandon drives, Microsoft OS, Epson printer, Taiwan display).

stem: Personal computers use a number of chips mounted on a main circuit board. What is the common name for such boards?
explanation: The main circuit board that carries the chips in a PC is the motherboard. A daughter board is a smaller board that plugs into the motherboard rather than being the main board, and a breadboard is a solderless prototyping board, not a production PC board.

stem: In most IBM PCs, the CPU, the device drives, memory expansion slots and active components are mounted on a single board. What is the name of this board?
explanation: The single board carrying the CPU, memory expansion slots, drive interfaces, and active components is the motherboard, by definition the main board everything else attaches to. Same fact as the previous item, asked from the component-list direction.

stem: What is meant by a dedicated computer?

options.label: A
options.text: Which is used by one person only

options.label: B
options.text: Which is assigned one and only one task

options.label: C
options.text: Which uses one kind of software

options.label: D
options.text: Which is meant for application software

explanation: A dedicated computer is one assigned to a single task or function, for example an embedded controller or a dedicated word processor. 'Used by one person only' is the trap: that is the definition of a personal computer, which can still run any number of different tasks.

stem: The system unit of a personal computer typically contains all of the following except:
explanation: The system unit houses the microprocessor, the disk controller, and the serial interface, so those three are all inside; the modem is the exception because it is classically an external device (or an add-on card) that connects the machine to a phone line. Note this is a dated question: internal modem cards do exist, but the exam key treats the modem as the outside-the-box item.

stem: A computer program that converts an entire program into machine language is called a/an
explanation: A compiler translates the entire source program into machine code in one pass before any of it runs, producing an object or executable file. The interpreter distractor is the direct contrast: it works one statement at a time (see the next question).

stem: A computer program that translates one program instructions at a time into machine language is called a/an
explanation: An interpreter reads, translates, and executes one instruction at a time, with no separate whole-program translation step and no executable file left behind. This is the exact complement of the compiler in the previous item: whole program at once = compiler, one statement at a time = interpreter.

stem: A small or intelligent device is so called because it contains within it a
explanation: Answer unverified: the circulated key marks D (Sensor), but the review panel resolved to B (Microcomputer). A device is called smart or intelligent because it embeds computation, that is a microcomputer (microprocessor plus memory and I/O) small enough to fit inside it; a sensor only transduces a physical quantity and confers no intelligence on its own. If the grader follows the printed key, expect D; on the merits the answer is B.

stem: The ALU of a computer responds to the commands coming from
explanation: The control unit decodes each instruction and issues the control signals that tell the ALU which operation to perform, so the ALU responds to the control section. Memory (primary, cache, or external) only supplies operands and instructions; it does not command the ALU.

stem: The act of retrieving existing data from memory is called
